theories.summary: In September 1984, followers of the Rajneesh commune, led by Ma Anand Sheela, deliberately contaminated salad bars at ten restaurants in The Dalles, Oregon with Salmonella bacteria, sickening 751 people in an attempt to suppress local voter turnout ahead of county elections. It remains the largest bioterrorism attack in U.S. history; investigators later found matching bacterial cultures in the commune's medical laboratory, and Sheela and an accomplice were convicted.

evidence_for.text: Wikipedia documents the salad-bar contamination, the 751 illnesses, the electoral motive, and the 1986 convictions of Ma Anand Sheela and Diane Yvonne Onang.
A peer-reviewed Cureus article confirms the attack as the largest bioterrorism episode in U.S. history, contaminating salad bars with Salmonella typhimurium and sickening over 750 people.

evidence_against: None -- documented historical fact, confirmed by criminal convictions and physical evidence recovered from the commune's laboratory.

sources.title: 1984 Rajneeshee bioterror attack
The 1984 Rajneeshee Bioterrorism Attack: An Example of Biological Warfare by Violent Non-state Actors

theories.name: Libor Scandal
theories.summary: Major international banks, including Barclays, UBS, and Deutsche Bank, systematically submitted false interest-rate data to manipulate the London Interbank Offered Rate (Libor), a benchmark underpinning hundreds of trillions of dollars in financial contracts, for profit and to appear more creditworthy. The scheme drew scrutiny from a 2008 Wall Street Journal investigation and was confirmed in June 2012 when Barclays admitted misconduct and paid regulatory penalties, triggering a wave of criminal prosecutions and multibillion-dollar fines against other banks.

evidence_for.text: Barclays admitted to the U.S. Department of Justice that it submitted false Libor and Euribor rates that improperly reflected its derivatives traders' positions, and paid a $160 million penalty.
Wikipedia documents that UBS, RBS, Deutsche Bank, Rabobank and others subsequently paid billions of dollars in fines, and UK trader Tom Hayes was criminally convicted for orchestrating the rigging.

evidence_against.text: Some individual convictions were later overturned on appeal — U.S. courts vacated Matthew Connolly's and Gavin Black's convictions in 2022, and the UK Supreme Court quashed Tom Hayes' and Carlo Palombo's convictions in 2025 over faulty jury instructions — though the banks' admissions of institutional wrongdoing and the fines stand.

sources.title: Libor scandal
Barclays Bank PLC Admits Misconduct Related to Submissions for the London Interbank Offered Rate and the Euro Interbank Offered Rate and Agrees to Pay $160 Million Penalty

theories.name: Forex Rigging Scandal
theories.summary: Traders at Barclays, Citigroup, JPMorgan, RBS, UBS and other major banks colluded through private chat rooms nicknamed "The Cartel" to front-run client orders and rig the WM/Reuters foreign-exchange benchmark rates for profit. Bloomberg News broke the story in June 2013, and in May 2015 five banks pleaded guilty to felony antitrust charges, paying more than $5.7 billion in combined fines.

evidence_for.text: The U.S. Department of Justice announced that Citicorp, JPMorgan Chase, Barclays and RBS agreed to plead guilty to conspiring to manipulate FX spot market prices, paying over $2.5 billion in criminal fines, while UBS pleaded guilty to a related Libor breach.
Wikipedia documents that at least 15 banks were implicated and that UK and US regulators separately levied over $3 billion in fines in November 2014.

evidence_against.text: Prosecutions of individual traders proved difficult; only a small number were criminally charged relative to the scale of the conspiracy.

sources.title: Forex scandal
Five Major Banks Agree to Parent-Level Guilty Pleas

theories.name: HSBC Money Laundering
theories.summary: HSBC failed to maintain adequate anti-money-laundering controls, allowing Mexico's Sinaloa and Norte del Valle drug cartels to launder at least $881 million through its U.S. accounts, and permitted transactions with sanctioned countries including Iran and Sudan. A July 2012 U.S. Senate investigation exposed the failures, and in December 2012 HSBC entered a deferred prosecution agreement and forfeited $1.92 billion.

evidence_for.text: The DOJ announced that HSBC Holdings and HSBC Bank USA admitted to anti-money-laundering and sanctions violations and forfeited $1.256 billion as part of a $1.92 billion settlement.
Wikipedia documents that HSBC Mexico was described by a recorded drug lord as a preferred location for laundering money, and that the bank failed to monitor $670 billion in wire transfers.

evidence_against.text: No HSBC executives were criminally prosecuted; the DOJ opted for a deferred prosecution agreement, citing concern that criminal charges could destabilize the bank and broader financial system.

sources.title: HSBC
HSBC Holdings Plc. and HSBC Bank USA N.A. Admit to Anti-Money Laundering and Sanctions Violations, Forfeit $1.256 Billion in Deferred Prosecution Agreement

theories.name: Wachovia Cartel Money Laundering
theories.summary: Wachovia Bank failed to monitor $378.4 billion in transfers with Mexican money-exchange houses between 2004 and 2007, allowing drug cartels to launder at least $110 million in narcotics proceeds through the U.S. financial system. First reported by the Wall Street Journal in 2008, the scheme was confirmed in March 2010 when Wachovia entered a deferred prosecution agreement and paid $160 million.

evidence_for.text: The U.S. Attorney's Office for the Southern District of Florida announced Wachovia's deferred prosecution agreement, under which the bank forfeited $110 million and paid a $50 million Treasury fine for what regulators called "systemic" Bank Secrecy Act violations.
Wikipedia documents that at least $373 billion in wire transfers and $4 billion in bulk cash moved through the casas de cambio accounts with insufficient anti-money-laundering controls.

evidence_against.text: As with other bank money-laundering cases of the era, no individual Wachovia executives faced criminal charges.

sources.title: Wachovia
Wachovia Enters Into Deferred Prosecution Agreement

theories.name: Deutsche Bank Russian Mirror Trades
theories.summary: Deutsche Bank's Moscow equities desk executed "mirror trades" — matched purchases in rubles and offsetting sales in other currencies through its London and New York branches — that moved roughly $10 billion out of Russia between 2011 and 2015, likely for money laundering or illicit capital flight. Financial journalists exposed the scheme in 2015, and in January 2017 the New York State Department of Financial Services fined the bank $425 million for the compliance failures.

evidence_for.text: The New York Department of Financial Services announced a $425 million fine and independent monitor requirement after finding Deutsche Bank's Moscow, London and New York offices ran a mirror-trading scheme that laundered $10 billion out of Russia.
Wikipedia documents the scheme operated by matching purchases of Russian blue-chip stocks in rubles with identical offsetting sales through the bank's London branch.

evidence_against.text: Investigators could not always identify the ultimate beneficiaries or definitively prove criminal intent behind every trade; the settlement was a civil/regulatory consent order rather than a criminal conviction.

sources.title: Deutsche Bank
DFS Fines Deutsche Bank $425 Million for Russian Mirror-Trading Scheme

theories.name: Purdue Pharma OxyContin Marketing
theories.summary: Purdue Pharma trained sales representatives to falsely tell doctors that OxyContin's addiction risk was under one percent and marketed 12-hour dosing despite internal data showing the drug often wore off sooner, fueling widespread overprescription and addiction. In May 2007, Purdue and three executives pleaded guilty to federal charges of misbranding the drug, and the company later faced an $8 billion global resolution and bankruptcy amid the broader opioid epidemic.

evidence_for.text: Internal Purdue documents and trial evidence show the company trained sales reps to claim OxyContin's addiction risk was under 1%, and in May 2007 Purdue Frederick pleaded guilty to felony misbranding, paying $600 million with three executives fined $34.5 million.

The Justice Department announced a further global resolution of criminal and civil investigations into Purdue Pharma's opioid marketing, part of the broader $8 billion settlement following the company's 2019 bankruptcy filing.

evidence_against.text: Members of the Sackler family, who owned and directed Purdue, largely avoided personal criminal liability despite the 2020 global resolution and later bankruptcy settlement.

sources.title: Purdue Pharma
Justice Department Announces Global Resolution of Criminal and Civil Investigations with Opioid Manufacturer Purdue Pharma and Civil Settlement with Members of the Sackler Family

theories.name: Insys Therapeutics Opioid Bribery
theories.summary: Insys Therapeutics paid doctors sham "speaker fees" as kickbacks to prescribe its fentanyl spray Subsys to non-cancer patients, and had employees impersonate physicians' offices to trick insurers into covering the drug. Federal prosecutors arrested founder John Kapoor and six other executives in December 2016; a 2019 jury convicted five of them of racketeering conspiracy, and the company paid a $225 million settlement before filing for bankruptcy.

evidence_for.text: The Justice Department announced a $225 million global resolution with Insys Therapeutics, including a guilty plea to mail fraud and a deferred prosecution agreement, over kickbacks paid to induce Subsys prescriptions.
Wikipedia documents that a May 2019 federal jury convicted founder John Kapoor and four other executives of racketeering conspiracy, and Kapoor was sentenced to 5.5 years in prison.

evidence_against.text: None — documented via federal jury conviction and DOJ settlement.

sources.title: Insys Therapeutics
Opioid Manufacturer Insys Therapeutics Agrees to Enter $225 Million Global Resolution of Criminal and Civil Investigations

theories.name: GlaxoSmithKline Bribery Scandal
theories.summary: GlaxoSmithKline funneled roughly 3 billion yuan (about $490 million) in bribes to Chinese doctors, hospitals, and officials through more than 700 travel agencies and consulting firms to boost drug sales. Chinese police revealed the scheme in 2013, and in September 2014 a Chinese court fined GSK $490 million — the largest corporate fine in Chinese history at the time — and gave the company's former China head, Mark Reilly, a suspended prison sentence.

evidence_for.text: Chinese authorities announced GSK had funneled HK$3.8 billion in kickbacks to doctors, hospitals and managers since 2007 using shell travel agencies and consulting firms.
A Chinese court fined GlaxoSmithKline $492 million for bribery, the largest corporate fine imposed in China at the time, and convicted former China head Mark Reilly.

evidence_against.text: Reilly and his Chinese co-defendants received suspended sentences and were never imprisoned, leading critics to call the punishment lenient relative to the scale of the bribery.

sources.title: GlaxoSmithKline
GlaxoSmithKline fined $492M by China in bribery scandal

theories.name: Merck Vioxx Scandal
theories.summary: Merck withheld and downplayed clinical trial data showing its painkiller Vioxx (rofecoxib) roughly quadrupled the risk of heart attack, continuing to market the drug to tens of millions of patients before voluntarily withdrawing it in September 2004 after further study confirmed the danger. Merck later pleaded guilty to a criminal misbranding charge and paid $950 million in fines and settlements, on top of a separate $4.85 billion mass-tort settlement for injured patients.

evidence_for.text: The Justice Department announced Merck Sharp & Dohme's guilty plea to illegally promoting Vioxx and a $950 million payment, including a $321.6 million criminal fine, for misrepresenting the drug's cardiovascular risks.

Wikipedia documents that the New England Journal of Medicine found critical cardiovascular adverse-event data were omitted from the published VIGOR trial results, and that an FDA analyst estimated the drug caused 88,000 to 140,000 heart attacks.

evidence_against.text: Merck maintained the withdrawal and settlements were not admissions that Vioxx caused any individual patient's heart attack, and causation in specific cases remained contested in litigation.

sources.title: Rofecoxib
U.S. Pharmaceutical Company Merck Sharp & Dohme to Pay Nearly One Billion Dollars Over Promotion of Vioxx

theories.name: Volkswagen Dieselgate
theories.summary: Volkswagen installed "defeat device" software in roughly 11 million diesel vehicles worldwide that detected laboratory emissions testing and activated full pollution controls only during the test, allowing cars to emit up to 40 times the legal nitrogen-oxide limit during normal driving. Independent researchers funded by the International Council on Clean Transportation uncovered the discrepancy in 2014, and the EPA issued a formal Notice of Violation in September 2015, leading to Volkswagen's guilty plea and over $33 billion in fines, penalties and buybacks.

evidence_for.text: The EPA's Notice of Violation to Volkswagen documented that software on 2009-2015 diesel models detected test conditions and enabled full emissions controls only during testing, evading Clean Air Act standards.
Volkswagen pleaded guilty to criminal charges in January 2017 and was ordered to pay a $2.8 billion criminal fine, part of over $33 billion in total costs by 2020.

evidence_against.text: None — Volkswagen admitted the defeat device scheme and pleaded guilty to criminal charges.

sources.title: Volkswagen emissions scandal
Learn About Volkswagen Violations

theories.name: Eurabia
theories.summary: Coined by writer Bat Ye'or (Gisèle Littman) and detailed in her 2005 book 'Eurabia: The Euro-Arab Axis,' this theory claims that European elites, in a secret 1970s pact trading Middle Eastern oil access for anti-Israel policies, deliberately opened Europe to mass Muslim immigration in order to Islamize and 'Arabize' the continent, replacing its culture and severing its alliance with the U.S. and Israel.
evidence_for: The real Euro-Arab Dialogue, a series of official EEC-Arab League diplomatic and economic talks, began in the mid-1970s following the 1973 oil crisis
European immigration from North Africa and the Middle East did increase substantially in the postwar decades, a real demographic trend
European governments genuinely sought closer ties with Arab oil-producing states after the 1973 embargo, which is documented diplomatic history
Some European officials have made public remarks about accommodating Muslim immigrant populations that critics cite as corroboration
evidence_against: The Euro-Arab Dialogue was an open, publicly documented diplomatic and trade forum, not a secret pact to engineer immigration or cultural replacement
Immigration to Europe resulted from decolonization, labor shortages, guest-worker programs, and asylum law, not a coordinated demographic plot
No credible historian has found evidence of any elite agreement to trade Israel policy for engineered Islamization
The theory has been criticized by scholars as structurally identical to the antisemitic 'Protocols of the Elders of Zion' template, substituting 'Arab/Muslim elites' for 'Jewish elites'
It has been cited approvingly in the manifesto of Anders Breivik, the 2011 Norway mass murderer, underscoring its real-world violence linkage

theories.name: Love Jihad
theories.summary: Originating among Hindu nationalist and Christian groups in Kerala around 2009, this theory claims that Muslim men systematically feign romantic interest in Hindu and Christian women as an organized strategy to convert them to Islam and shift India's religious demographics; it has since spread across India and inspired state-level 'anti-conversion' legislation.
evidence_for: Individual interfaith relationships and religious conversions following marriage genuinely occur in India, as anywhere
The Kerala Catholic Bishops' Council publicly claimed thousands of Christian women had been targeted, giving the narrative institutional backing
A handful of individual criminal cases involving deception in interfaith relationships have been prosecuted and are cited as proof
Several Indian states have passed 'anti-love-jihad' or unlawful-conversion laws, which proponents cite as validation
evidence_against: Indian police investigations — including a formal 2009 Kerala inquiry and a 2017-18 National Investigation Agency probe into the high-profile Hadiya case — found no evidence of any organized conversion conspiracy
Kerala police stated outright in 2012 there was 'no substance' to the love jihad claims after investigating dozens of alleged cases
Academic studies of interfaith marriage statistics show no unusual pattern consistent with an organized campaign
The theory functions as a moral panic that researchers say has been used to justify vigilante violence and restrictions on women's autonomy
The Hadiya case, held up as its most prominent 'proof,' ended with the Supreme Court of India upholding the marriage as the free choice of a consenting adult

theories.name: Zionist Occupation Government (ZOG)
theories.summary: First coined in a 1976 article by neo-Nazi Eric Thomson, 'ZOG' claims that the U.S. and other Western governments are secretly controlled behind the scenes by a coordinated Jewish cabal, with elected officials serving merely as public-facing puppets. The term was adopted by white-supremacist groups including the Order and Posse Comitatus and remains current in neo-Nazi and Christian Identity circles. (Catalogued to document a violent, foundational antisemitic canard, not to endorse it.)
evidence_for: Jewish individuals, like members of many other groups, hold prominent positions in Western finance, media, and politics — a fact ZOG proponents distort into evidence of coordinated ethnic control
Proponents cite texts like the 'Protocols of the Elders of Zion' as if they were authentic evidence, despite the Protocols being a proven forgery
evidence_against: No credible evidence of any coordinated Jewish control of any Western government exists; the claim requires treating the ordinary presence of Jewish individuals in public life as proof of ethnic conspiracy, a logical fallacy
The 'Protocols of the Elders of Zion,' frequently cited as source material, was proven a forgery by the Times of London in 1921, plagiarized from an 1864 political satire unrelated to Jews

The theory directly motivated real terrorist violence, including the 1984 assassination of Denver radio host Alan Berg by members of the neo-Nazi group the Order, and continues to appear in mass-shooter manifestos
Western governments are demonstrably fractious and often act against policies any single ethnic lobby would prefer, contradicting the premise of unified covert control
Mainstream historians classify ZOG as a direct descendant of medieval and modern antisemitic conspiracy myths with no basis in verifiable fact

theories.name: Holocaust Denial
theories.summary: Holocaust denial claims that the systematic genocide of six million European Jews by Nazi Germany either did not occur, was greatly exaggerated, lacked centralized extermination intent, or has been fabricated by Jewish groups for political gain. Early individual deniers such as Paul Rassinier emerged in the late 1940s, and the movement was institutionalized with the 1978 founding of the Institute for Historical Review. (Catalogued solely to document and refute a hateful, historically false ideology; not an endorsement.)
evidence_for: Deniers cite disputed post-war forensic reports and the destruction of some camp records by retreating Germans as grounds to claim uncertainty about death-camp mechanics

Deniers point to genuine early revisions in death-toll estimates for specific camps (which historians themselves refined with better records over time) and misrepresent normal historical refinement as proof of fabrication

Deniers cite the absence of a single written Hitler order for total extermination — historians attribute this to verbal orders and euphemistic bureaucratic language, well documented via other means — to claim lack of top-down intent
evidence_against: The Holocaust is among the most thoroughly documented events in history: Nazi Germany's own administrative records, deportation lists, camp blueprints, and the minutes of the 1942 Wannsee Conference explicitly describe the genocidal program

Physical evidence at Auschwitz-Birkenau, Treblinka, and other camps — including gas chamber remains, crematoria, and mass graves — has been forensically documented by multiple independent international investigations since 1945

Tens of thousands of survivor testimonies, perpetrator confessions (including at the Nuremberg and Eichmann trials), and Allied liberation footage corroborate the same account across independent sources

Death-toll estimate refinements reflect normal historiographic progress with more complete records, not fabrication — the consensus figure of roughly 6 million Jewish victims (out of about 11 million total Nazi victims) has been stable in serious scholarship for decades
Prominent deniers, notably David Irving in the 2000 Irving v. Lipstadt libel trial, have had their claims examined in court and were found to be deliberate distortions of the historical record
Holocaust denial is criminalized in numerous countries specifically because of its demonstrated role in inciting antisemitic violence and Nazi rehabilitation

theories.name: Blood Libel
theories.summary: Beginning with the 1144 death of a boy, William, in Norwich, England, the blood libel is the false medieval and modern accusation that Jews ritually murder Christian (or other non-Jewish) children to use their blood in religious rites, especially around Passover. Monk Thomas of Monmouth popularized the Norwich accusation years after the unsolved death, and the trope recurred across Europe for centuries, repeatedly triggering pogroms. (Catalogued to document a historically lethal antisemitic myth, not to endorse it.)
evidence_for: Proponents historically pointed to unexplained child deaths occurring near Jewish communities and to coerced confessions extracted under torture as 'proof'
Some later hagiographic accounts (e.g., Thomas of Monmouth's narrative of William of Norwich) were treated as authoritative religious texts for centuries, lending the myth institutional credibility
evidence_against: No case of ritual child murder by Jews has ever been substantiated by physical evidence in nearly 900 years of recurring accusations
William of Norwich's death in 1144 had no evidence linking Jews to it at the time; the accusation was invented four years later by Thomas of Monmouth with no eyewitness or forensic basis
Jewish religious law explicitly and historically prohibits consuming blood in any form, directly contradicting the premise of the myth
Numerous confessions used to 'confirm' the libel were extracted under torture, a method known to produce false statements matching whatever narrative the torturer suggests
Multiple Popes, including Innocent IV in 1247, issued formal declarations rejecting the blood libel as false even in the medieval period
The myth directly caused the killing of Jewish communities across Europe for centuries and was later revived by the Nazis in propaganda like Der Stürmer

theories.name: Well Poisoning / Black Death Jewish Conspiracy
theories.summary: During the Black Death, a rumor arose in Switzerland in autumn 1348 claiming that Jews had conspired internationally to poison Christian wells and cause the plague; confessions extracted under torture from Jews near Lake Geneva spread the accusation down the Rhine, triggering massacres of Jewish communities across dozens of towns, including the annihilation of the entire Jewish populations of Basel and Strasbourg in early 1349. (Catalogued to document a historically genocidal antisemitic myth, not to endorse it.)
evidence_for: Proponents at the time pointed to confessions obtained from arrested Jews as 'proof' the conspiracy was real
The rapid, terrifying, and poorly understood spread of plague created genuine public panic and a demand for an explanatory scapegoat that authorities and mobs found in existing antisemitic tropes
evidence_against: Pope Clement VI issued formal bulls in 1348 declaring the well-poisoning accusation false, noting that Jews were dying of the plague in the same proportions as Christians — clear evidence against a Jewish immunity or conspiracy
The confessions used as 'proof' were extracted under judicial torture, a method documented even at the time to produce false statements
Modern epidemiology confirms the Black Death was caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis, spread primarily via fleas on rodents, with no possible causal link to well contamination

The scale claimed — a coordinated, continent-wide poisoning campaign by scattered, mutually unconnected small communities — is logistically implausible, and no physical evidence of poisoning was ever produced

The accusation directly caused the massacre of thousands of Jews in over 200 towns, including the burning alive of the entire Jewish communities of Basel and Strasbourg in 1349 — a documented historical atrocity

theories.name: Jewish Bolshevism
theories.summary: Emerging around the 1917 Russian Revolution and heavily promoted by Nazi ideologues like Alfred Rosenberg, this theory claims that Communism was fundamentally a Jewish plot to destroy Christian civilization and seize world power, pointing to the ethnicity of some prominent early Bolsheviks such as Leon Trotsky. The myth was central to Nazi propaganda and used to justify the mass murder of Jews across Eastern Europe during World War II. (Catalogued to document a genocidal historical trope, not to endorse it.)
evidence_for: Some individual Bolshevik and early Soviet figures, including Trotsky (born Lev Bronstein), were of Jewish descent, a real fact conspiracists inflate into evidence of ethnic coordination

Jewish communities, subject to Tsarist pogroms and legal discrimination, were disproportionately represented in revolutionary and socialist movements generally, which proponents misconstrue as proof of an organized ethnic plot rather than a response to persecution
evidence_against: The overwhelming majority of Bolshevik leadership, including Lenin and Stalin, were not Jewish, and Jewish communities were themselves major victims of Soviet repression, purges, and later Stalinist antisemitic campaigns (e.g., the 1952 Night of the Murdered Poets, the 'Doctors' Plot')

Historians attribute Jewish participation in revolutionary movements to specific socio-political conditions — Tsarist-era pogroms, the Pale of Settlement, exclusion from mainstream institutions — not ethnic coordination
The theory was fabricated and weaponized primarily by Nazi ideologues (Alfred Rosenberg) to justify the Holocaust, and historians universally regard it as propaganda rather than analysis
Communism took root and was independently embraced across dozens of countries with negligible Jewish populations (China, Vietnam, Cuba), directly contradicting a coordinated Jewish origin
The myth is a direct descendant of, and frequently cross-referenced with, the fabricated 'Protocols of the Elders of Zion'

theories.name: Khazarian Mafia
theories.summary: A modern online conspiracy theory claiming that a secret cabal descended from the medieval Khazar Khaganate — falsely framed as 'not real Jews' — controls global banking, media, and politics; it draws on a distorted version of the historical Khazar conversion narrative popularized by Arthur Koestler's 1976 book 'The Thirteenth Tribe' and crystallized on fringe outlets like Veterans Today around 2010, later amplified during the 2022 Russia-Ukraine war.
evidence_for: The Khazar Khaganate was a real medieval Turkic state whose ruling elite, according to several medieval sources, adopted Judaism — a genuine and debated historical episode conspiracists distort into their modern claim

Arthur Koestler's 1976 popular book speculated (controversially, and since largely discredited by genetic and historical scholarship) that many Ashkenazi Jews descend from Khazar converts, giving the theory a veneer of scholarly origin
evidence_against: Modern genetic studies of Ashkenazi Jewish populations show predominantly Middle Eastern ancestry with limited evidence of significant Khazar admixture, contradicting the Khazar-descent premise itself

Historians note the scale, and even the basic historicity, of a full Khazar elite conversion is contested and, regardless, provides no basis for claims about the ancestry or coordination of any group today

No evidence supports the existence of any 'Khazarian Mafia' controlling global finance, media, or politics; the claim functions as a rebranding of the older Rothschild/ZOG banking-control canards using invented pseudo-genetic language

The theory has been weaponized in Russian state-aligned propaganda to delegitimize Ukrainian sovereignty and to malign both Jews and Ukrainians simultaneously, per multiple hate-monitoring organizations
Its primary promotional outlets (e.g., Veterans Today) have been repeatedly identified by researchers and journalists as unreliable fringe publications with no vetted sourcing

theories.name: Barack Obama Birther Theory
theories.summary: The birther theory claimed Barack Obama was not born in the United States (usually alleging Kenya) and was therefore constitutionally ineligible to serve as president; it began circulating via anonymous 2008 chain emails, was amplified by figures including Donald Trump from 2011, and persisted despite Hawaii's release of both a short-form certification and, in 2011, the full long-form birth certificate.
evidence_for: Obama's father was Kenyan, and Obama spent part of his childhood in Indonesia and had international family ties, which proponents pointed to as suspicious
The document released in 2008 was initially a 'Certification of Live Birth' (a short-form abstract), which proponents argued was insufficient proof, fueling calls for the original long-form document
Some early promotional biographical materials (e.g., a 1991 literary agency booklet) erroneously described Obama as 'born in Kenya,' which proponents seized on as an admission
evidence_against: The Hawaii Department of Health confirmed contemporaneous 1961 birth records, and Hawaii officials of both parties repeatedly verified Obama was born in Honolulu

Two Honolulu newspapers published birth announcements for Obama in August 1961, based on official state birth notifications — records that could not have been fabricated decades later for a future presidential candidacy
Obama released the full long-form birth certificate in April 2011, which independent forensic document examiners found consistent with genuine Hawaii records of that era
The 1991 literary-booklet error was confirmed by the agency to be an unauthorized promotional simplification, not sourced from Obama himself
No court, election board, or investigative body that examined birther claims — including numerous state-level lawsuits — found any merit to them

theories.name: Obama Secret Muslim Theory
theories.summary: This theory claims Barack Obama secretly practices or sympathizes with Islam and concealed this from voters, citing his middle name Hussein, his Indonesian childhood schooling, and his father's and stepfather's nominal Muslim backgrounds. It spread via chain emails from at least 2004, was amplified by a false, unsourced 2007 Insight Magazine 'madrassa' story, and persisted throughout his presidency despite his lifelong public Christian affiliation.
evidence_for: Obama's father and stepfather were both raised at least nominally Muslim, and Obama's middle name (Hussein) and years living in Muslim-majority Indonesia as a child are real biographical facts proponents cite
Obama attended a public elementary school in Jakarta for several years, which some early reports mischaracterized as an Islamic religious school
evidence_against: CNN and other outlets sent reporters to the Jakarta school Obama attended and confirmed it was a secular public school, not a madrassa, contradicting the central claim of the 2007 Insight Magazine story

The Insight Magazine report was published without a single named source and without independent verification, and was subsequently discredited by The Washington Post, LA Times, and Chicago Tribune reporting
Obama has been a practicing Christian throughout his adult public life, was married in a Christian church, and attended Trinity United Church of Christ in Chicago for two decades
A person's family religious background or childhood schooling does not determine their own adult religious identity, which Obama has consistently and publicly stated is Christian

Pew Research polling documented the belief was strongly correlated with partisan opposition rather than with any new factual evidence, indicating a politically motivated rather than evidence-driven claim

theories.name: Clinton Body Count
theories.summary: This theory alleges that Bill and/or Hillary Clinton ordered or orchestrated the deaths of dozens of associates, aides, and witnesses — most prominently deputy White House counsel Vince Foster — to cover up crimes ranging from Arkansas-era financial dealings to later scandals. It originated with a 1993 list compiled by activist Linda Thompson and was circulated in Congress in 1994 by Rep. William Dannemeyer, later amplified by the 'Clinton Chronicles' video and repeatedly revived (e.g., around Jeffrey Epstein's 2019 death).
evidence_for: Vince Foster, a deputy White House counsel and close Clinton friend, was found dead of a gunshot wound in July 1993 amid the Whitewater investigation, a genuinely tragic and politically fraught death
Several people on various circulated 'body count' lists did have some real, documented connection to the Clintons, which proponents present as suspicious in aggregate
Real political scandals surrounding the Clintons (Whitewater, campaign finance issues) created a genuine backdrop of scrutiny that lent circumstantial plausibility to some observers
evidence_against: Five separate official investigations, including two independent counsel inquiries, concluded Vince Foster's death was a suicide linked to documented depression and workplace stress, with forensic evidence consistent with that finding
List compiler Linda Thompson herself admitted she had no direct evidence connecting the Clintons to any of the listed deaths

Many names on various circulating lists include people who died of natural causes, accidents, or illness with no plausible link to the Clintons at all, and some entries have been shown to be misattributed or still living
No physical evidence, credible witness, or successful prosecution has ever connected the Clintons to any of the alleged deaths

The theory has persisted for three decades specifically because it is unfalsifiable — any death of anyone tangentially connected to the Clintons gets retroactively added to the list regardless of documented cause

theories.name: Seth Rich Murder Conspiracy
theories.summary: This theory claims that DNC staffer Seth Rich, killed in a July 2016 Washington D.C. robbery-homicide, was actually murdered in retaliation for leaking internal DNC emails to WikiLeaks — implying the 2016 email leak was an inside job rather than the confirmed Russian government hack, and that Rich's death was a political assassination and cover-up rather than a botched robbery.
evidence_for: Rich did work at the DNC during the period the emails were stolen, and his unsolved murder occurred shortly before WikiLeaks began publishing the leaked emails, a real temporal coincidence that fueled speculation
Police were initially unable to identify a robbery motive since Rich's belongings (wallet, watch, phone) were left on his body, an unusual detail for a robbery that proponents cite as suspicious
WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange made public comments implying, without confirming, that Rich could have been a source, which lent the theory an air of insider credibility
evidence_against: The July 2018 U.S. indictment of 12 Russian GRU intelligence officers, based on detailed forensic and signals evidence, documents the DNC hack as a Russian state operation, and the U.S. intelligence community independently reached the same conclusion
Rich's colleagues and supervisors confirmed he did not have access to the specific DNC servers and email archives that were breached, and had no background as a hacker

Washington D.C. police and the FBI investigated and found no evidence linking Rich's death to the DNC leak; the case remains consistent with an unsolved local robbery-homicide, of which interrupted robberies are not unusual

Multiple purveyors of the theory — including Fox News, commentator Ed Butowsky, and journalist Matt Couch — retracted their claims and issued formal apologies or paid settlements to the Rich family (2020-2021) after admitting the reporting was false

Assange never definitively confirmed Rich as a source, and WikiLeaks' vague statements were widely noted by fact-checkers as consistent with protecting real sources' anonymity rather than confirming Rich's involvement

theories.name: 2020 Stolen Election ("Stop the Steal")
theories.summary: This theory holds that the 2020 U.S. presidential election was stolen from Donald Trump through coordinated mass voter fraud, rigged voting machines, and illegal ballot manipulation across multiple swing states. Promoted under the banner 'Stop the Steal' (a name Roger Stone had used since 2016), it drove over 60 lawsuits, state audits and recounts, and ultimately the January 6, 2021 Capitol attack.
evidence_for: Pandemic-driven expansion of mail-in voting in 2020 created genuinely new procedures in many states, which proponents argued increased fraud opportunity even though officials had implemented safeguards
Isolated, individually prosecuted instances of voter fraud did occur in the 2020 election, as in essentially every U.S. election, and were cited by proponents as evidence of a broader pattern

Statistical anomalies and vote-counting pauses on election night — a product of different states' differing mail-ballot counting timelines — appeared suspicious to observers unfamiliar with those procedures
Some local officials and poll watchers raised procedural concerns or filed affidavits alleging irregularities, which proponents treated as substantiating evidence
evidence_against: More than 60 lawsuits filed by Trump and allies were rejected by courts, including by numerous Trump-appointed judges, for lack of evidence
Georgia conducted a full hand recount of every ballot, and Arizona's Maricopa County underwent a Trump-ally-commissioned 'audit' (the Cyber Ninjas review); both confirmed Biden's win

Trump's own Justice Department (under AG Bill Barr) and CISA (the federal cybersecurity agency) stated they found no evidence of fraud sufficient to change the outcome, with CISA calling it 'the most secure election in American history'
Individually prosecuted fraud cases numbered only in the dozens nationwide, several orders of magnitude too few to have affected a result decided by tens of thousands of votes in each contested state

Forensic reviews of voting machines (including Dominion systems) found no evidence of vote manipulation or foreign interference; defamation lawsuits over false machine-fraud claims resulted in a $787 million settlement paid by Fox News to Dominion in 2023
The theory directly motivated the January 6, 2021 Capitol attack, for which several hundred participants have since been criminally convicted

theories.name: January 6 False Flag Theory
theories.summary: This theory holds that the January 6, 2021 Capitol riot was secretly orchestrated or incited by federal law enforcement — most often the FBI — using undercover agents or informants posing as Trump supporters (commonly naming Arizona man Ray Epps) in order to entrap and discredit the broader Trump movement, rather than being a genuine, organic riot by Trump supporters contesting the election.
evidence_for: Ray Epps was filmed on January 5-6, 2021 encouraging people to enter the Capitol, then was seen at the perimeter fence, behavior proponents argued resembled an agitator role
Epps briefly appeared on the FBI's most-wanted list for the riot and was later quietly removed without being charged for months, which fed suspicion of special treatment
The FBI is documented to use informants and undercover employees in extremist investigations generally, a real and acknowledged law-enforcement practice cited as a plausibility anchor
Prominent media figures (Tucker Carlson) and members of Congress gave the theory a platform, lending it an appearance of credibility to their audiences
evidence_against: The FBI has stated directly, and Epps has testified under oath to the House January 6 Committee, that he was never an FBI informant, agent, or asset before, during, or after the events
Epps was eventually criminally charged and pleaded guilty in 2023 to a misdemeanor for his conduct on January 6 — behavior inconsistent with someone protected as a government operative

Video and documentary evidence show the vast majority of the roughly 1,500 people later charged for January 6 were unaffiliated private citizens motivated by belief in the stolen-election claim, not federal operatives
No document, whistleblower, or piece of forensic evidence has substantiated any FBI role in inciting or orchestrating the riot

The theory caused severe real-world harm to Epps and his family, including death threats that forced them to sell their home and relocate — harm inflicted on a private citizen based on an unsubstantiated claim
theories.steelman: The FBI's documented history of embedding informants inside domestic political movements, as in COINTELPRO, shows the bureau has both the capability and institutional habit of monitoring groups it investigates from the inside. Congressional and inspector-general reviews specifically examined this claim for January 6 and found evidence of routine informant presence but not orchestration.

theories.name: BlueAnon
theories.summary: BlueAnon is a pejorative umbrella term, coined by conservative commentators as a rhetorical mirror of 'QAnon,' for a loose collection of conspiracy-tinged beliefs held by some on the political left — including claims that Donald Trump is a knowing Russian intelligence asset, that his 2024 assassination attempt was staged, or that Republican officials are secretly controlled by shadowy funders — gaining wide use after the 2016 Trump-Russia controversy and again around the 2024 election cycle.
evidence_for: Some underlying concerns have real, documented substance — the U.S. intelligence community did confirm Russian government interference in the 2016 election (though not proven coordination by the Trump campaign), giving surface plausibility to broader claims

Individual instances of politically motivated misinformation or exaggeration from left-leaning social media accounts and commentators are real and documented, which the term uses as its evidentiary base
The term highlights a genuine phenomenon: partisan communities across the spectrum are susceptible to unfalsifiable, motivated reasoning about opposing political figures
evidence_against: Unlike QAnon, 'BlueAnon' is not a single coherent belief system with a defined narrative or leader — it is a retroactive label applied by critics to a scattered grab-bag of unrelated claims of varying truth-value that share no common authorship or structure

Specific flagship claims under the BlueAnon umbrella — such as the 2024 Trump assassination attempt being staged — have been directly contradicted by law enforcement investigations, video evidence, and the shooter's independently confirmed death

The 2019 Mueller Report did not establish a criminal conspiracy between the Trump campaign and Russia, undercutting the strongest version of the 'Trump is a Russian asset' claim, even though it documented extensive contacts and confirmed Russian interference

Commentators note the QAnon comparison is largely rhetorical whataboutism — QAnon involved millions of adherents organized around a single anonymous prophetic figure with specific, repeatedly falsified predictions, a scale and structure 'BlueAnon' does not match

theories.name: Crisis Actors Theory
theories.summary: This theory claims that mass-casualty events, most often school shootings and terrorist attacks, are partly or wholly staged using paid 'crisis actors' — professionals who legitimately role-play in emergency-response training drills — to manufacture public support for gun control or other policy goals. It originated with a 2012 blog post by James Tracy about the Sandy Hook Elementary School shooting and was later applied to the 2013 Boston Marathon bombing and numerous subsequent tragedies.
evidence_for: 'Crisis actor' is a real job/role: trained actors are genuinely hired by emergency-management agencies to simulate victims during first-responder training drills, giving the term surface legitimacy
A real firm, Visionbox, did offer dramatic training in portraying disaster victims, which proponents pointed to as a literal 'agency' supposedly supplying shooting-scene performers

Public grief, media interviews, and minor factual inconsistencies in early, chaotic breaking-news coverage of any large tragedy are real phenomena that proponents misinterpret as evidence of scripting
evidence_against: No physical, financial, or documentary evidence has ever substantiated that any actual mass-casualty event (Sandy Hook, Boston Marathon, or any other cited case) used paid actors instead of real victims

Twenty real children and six adult staff members were killed at Sandy Hook Elementary in December 2012, confirmed by autopsies, funerals, death certificates, and extensive independent journalism and law-enforcement investigation

Alex Jones, the theory's most prominent promoter, admitted under oath during 2022 defamation trials that the Sandy Hook shooting was '100% real' after years of promoting the crisis-actor theory, and was found liable for roughly $1.5 billion in damages to victims' families
Grieving families targeted by the theory have been subjected to years of harassment, death threats, and stalking by believers, a well-documented real-world harm stemming directly from the false claim

The apparent 'inconsistencies' cited as proof are the normal artifacts of chaotic breaking-news reporting (misattributed photos, early wrong details later corrected) rather than evidence of a scripted event

theories.name: The Great Purge
theories.summary: Between 1936 and 1938, Stalin's regime carried out mass arrests, forced confessions, show trials, and extrajudicial executions of Communist Party members, military officers, and ordinary citizens through NKVD troikas. The scale of the killing was first publicly admitted in Khrushchev's 1956 'Secret Speech' and later fully confirmed by Soviet archives declassified after 1991.

evidence_for.text: Nikita Khrushchev's 1956 'Secret Speech' to the 20th Party Congress denounced Stalin's purges and cited NKVD archive materials showing fabricated cases against innocent people.
Declassified Soviet archives opened after 1991 document at least 681,692 executions in 1937-1938 alone, with scholarly death-toll estimates ranging from 700,000 to 1.2 million.

evidence_against: Precise overall death toll (700,000-1.2 million) still varies by source and methodology; the ethnic-targeting scope of some 'national operations' remains debated among historians.

sources.title: Great Purge
Khrushchev's Secret Speech, 1956

theories.name: Holodomor
theories.summary: Soviet forced collectivization and grain-requisition policies produced a catastrophic famine in Ukraine in 1932-1933 that killed an estimated 3.5 to 10 million people. Soviet authorities suppressed all public discussion of the famine for decades; open acknowledgment began during glasnost in the late 1980s, and the famine has since been recognized as genocide by Ukraine and over 30 other countries.

evidence_for.text: A 2003 Joint Statement at the United Nations, signed by 25+ member states including Russia, Ukraine, and the United States, formally acknowledged the Holodomor took 'from 7 million to 10 million innocent lives.'
Soviet-era censorship of the famine ended during glasnost, with open discussion appearing in Soviet publications by 1988.

evidence_against.text: Historians unanimously agree the famine was man-made, but disagree on whether Stalin specifically intended to target Ukrainians (genocide) versus an unintended consequence of broader Soviet industrialization and requisition policy.

sources.title: Holodomor
Joint Statement on Holodomor (UN, 2003)

theories.name: Tulsa Race Massacre (1921)
theories.summary: Over May 31-June 1, 1921, white mobs in Tulsa, Oklahoma attacked and destroyed the prosperous Greenwood District, known as 'Black Wall Street,' killing an estimated 39 to 300 Black residents and burning more than 35 city blocks. The massacre was largely omitted from official histories for decades until a state-authorized Oklahoma Commission investigated it and published a final report in 2001 recommending reparations.

evidence_for.text: The Oklahoma Commission to Study the Tulsa Race Riot of 1921, created by the state legislature in 1997, published its Final Report in February 2001 documenting the massacre and recommending reparations and educational reforms.
A 2001 forensic examination identified at least 39 confirmed deaths, with contemporary estimates ranging as high as approximately 300.

evidence_against: Exact death toll remains disputed; official 1921 records listed only 36 deaths while later investigations and mass-grave searches suggest a substantially higher toll.

sources.title: Tulsa race massacre
Tulsa Race Riot: A Report by the Oklahoma Commission to Study the Tulsa Race Riot of 1921 (Final Report, 2001)

theories.name: Political Abuse of Psychiatry in the USSR
aliases: Punitive Psychiatry in the Soviet Union
theories.summary: Soviet authorities systematically committed political dissidents to psychiatric hospitals under fabricated diagnoses such as 'sluggish schizophrenia,' subjecting an estimated 20,000 or more people to forced drugging, restraint, and indefinite detention in lieu of criminal prosecution. International psychiatric organizations documented and condemned the practice through the 1970s and 1980s, and the U.S. Congress held hearings on it, contributing to the USSR's 1983 withdrawal from the World Psychiatric Association.

evidence_for.text: The U.S. Commission on Security and Cooperation in Europe (Helsinki Commission) held a congressional hearing, 'Abuse of Psychiatry in the Soviet Union,' compiling testimony and documentation of the practice.

World Psychiatric Association congresses in 1971, 1977, and 1983 formally condemned Soviet psychiatric abuse; between 1988-1989 roughly one to two million people were removed from Soviet psychiatric registries as the USSR sought re-admission to the WPA.

evidence_against: The total number of victims is not precisely known; estimates range from a documented minimum of about 20,000 to considerably higher figures depending on the source.

sources.title: Political abuse of psychiatry in the Soviet Union
Abuse of Psychiatry in the Soviet Union (CSCE Congressional Hearing)

theories.name: Magdalene Laundries & Illegal Irish Adoptions
theories.summary: Catholic religious orders operated Magdalene Laundries in Ireland from the 18th century until 1996, confining an estimated 30,000 women and girls to perform unpaid labor, with documented cases of coerced and illegal adoption of their children. A 2013 Irish government inquiry, the McAleese Report, confirmed direct state involvement in referring at least 2,500 women to the institutions, prompting a formal state apology and a compensation scheme.

evidence_for.text: The Report of the Inter-Departmental Committee to Establish the Facts of State Involvement with the Magdalen Laundries (the McAleese Report), published 5 February 2013, found the state had direct involvement in committing over 2,500 women and that at least 10,000 women passed through the laundries.
Taoiseach Enda Kenny issued a formal state apology on 19 February 2013, calling the laundries 'the nation's shame.'

evidence_against: The full scope of illegal adoptions and the total number of unmarked graves remains incomplete, as some religious-order records were destroyed or withheld.

sources.title: Magdalene Laundries in Ireland
Report of the Inter-Departmental Committee to Establish the Facts of State Involvement with the Magdalen Laundries

theories.name: Federal Poisoning of Industrial Alcohol (Prohibition)
theories.summary: Beginning in 1926, the U.S. federal government required industrial alcohol to be adulterated with lethal chemicals, including methanol, in an effort to deter bootleggers from diverting it into illegal drinking alcohol. The policy killed an estimated 10,000 people by the end of Prohibition in 1933 and was publicly condemned at the time by New York City chief medical examiner Charles Norris, who called it a 'national experiment in extermination.'

evidence_for.text: The Wikipedia article on the consequences of Prohibition documents the federally mandated denaturing of industrial alcohol with methanol, pyridine, and benzene, and estimates it killed as many as 10,000 people.
Contemporary reporting recounts NYC medical examiner Charles Norris's public accusation that the government bore 'moral responsibility for the deaths that poisoned liquor causes.'

evidence_against: The commonly cited death toll of roughly 10,000 is a period estimate and cannot be independently verified against modern forensic recordkeeping standards.

sources.title: Consequences of Prohibition
The little-told story of how the U.S. government poisoned alcohol during Prohibition

theories.name: Sugar Research Foundation Cover-up
theories.summary: In the mid-1960s the Sugar Research Foundation paid Harvard scientists, including nutrition department chair Frederick Stare, to produce a literature review published in the New England Journal of Medicine that downplayed sugar's role in heart disease and shifted blame toward dietary fat, shaping U.S. nutrition guidance for decades. The industry's role was exposed in a 2016 JAMA Internal Medicine analysis of internal industry documents by researchers Cristin Kearns, Laura Schmidt, and Stanton Glantz.

evidence_for.text: Kearns, Schmidt, and Glantz's 2016 JAMA Internal Medicine paper, 'Sugar Industry and Coronary Heart Disease Research: A Historical Analysis of Internal Industry Documents,' shows the Sugar Research Foundation paid roughly $6,500 (about $50,000 in 2016 dollars) to Harvard researchers to produce a review minimizing sugar's cardiovascular risks.
The Sugar Research Foundation is documented as the funder and coordinator of this research program.

evidence_against.text: The Sugar Association, responding to the 2016 exposure, noted that financial-disclosure standards were far less stringent in the 1960s than today.

sources.title: Sugar Research Foundation
Sugar Industry and Coronary Heart Disease Research: A Historical Analysis of Internal Industry Documents

theories.name: NFL Concussion Research Interference
theories.summary: From 1994, the NFL's Mild Traumatic Brain Injury Committee, chaired by a rheumatologist with no neurology background, published research funded and staffed by the league concluding concussions posed little long-term risk, while omitting more than 10 percent of diagnosed concussions from its underlying data. Investigative journalism, including the 2013 book and PBS documentary 'League of Denial' and a 2016 New York Times investigation, exposed the flawed data and the league's yearslong public denial of a link between football and chronic brain injury.

evidence_for.text: The 2013 book and PBS Frontline documentary 'League of Denial' detailed how the NFL's concussion committee omitted more than 100 diagnosed concussions from its published data between 1996 and 2001.

A 2016 New York Times investigation, 'N.F.L.'s Flawed Concussion Research and Ties to Tobacco Industry,' found the concussion data used in the league's papers was significantly and likely knowingly incomplete.

evidence_against: The NFL disputed characterizations of deliberate data suppression and demanded a retraction of the 2016 New York Times story; the league has since funded independent concussion research and adopted new safety protocols.

sources.title: Concussions in American football
N.F.L.'s Flawed Concussion Research and Ties to Tobacco Industry

theories.name: Iraq WMD Justification
theories.summary: The United States and United Kingdom justified the 2003 invasion of Iraq by asserting that Saddam Hussein possessed active chemical, biological, and nuclear weapons programs. The U.S.-led Iraq Survey Group's 2004 Duelfer Report found no WMD stockpiles had existed since 1991, and the UK's 2016 Chilcot Inquiry concluded the war was launched on intelligence presented with unwarranted certainty and without exhausting peaceful alternatives.

evidence_for.text: The Iraq Survey Group's final report (the Duelfer Report), submitted to Congress on 30 September 2004, reaffirmed that Iraq did not possess stockpiles of chemical, biological, or nuclear weapons at the time of the 2003 invasion.
The UK's 2016 Chilcot Inquiry (Iraq Inquiry) found that intelligence on Iraqi WMD was presented with unwarranted certainty and that the case for war was flawed.

evidence_against: The Iraq Survey Group also found Saddam Hussein retained the intent to revive WMD programs once UN sanctions were lifted, though no active program or stockpile existed at the time of invasion.

sources.title: Iraq and weapons of mass destruction
Iraq Survey Group Final Report (Duelfer Report), 2004
Iraq Inquiry (Chilcot Report)

theories.name: CPI Inflation Understatement (ShadowStats)
aliases: Real inflation rate is double the official rate
theories.summary: Claims the Bureau of Labor Statistics deliberately understates the Consumer Price Index — via hedonic quality adjustments, geometric weighting, and substitution — to shrink cost-of-living payouts for Social Security, disability benefits, and TIPS bondholders, and to make administrations look better on inflation. Popularized by John Williams' ShadowStats, which during the 2021-2023 inflation surge claimed 'real' inflation running two to three times the official rate. Economists who have audited BLS methodology find the accumulated effect of methodology revisions since the 1980s amounts to a fraction of a percentage point, not the multi-point gap ShadowStats claims, and Williams has acknowledged he adds an estimated offset to the official figure rather than recomputing inflation with historical formulas.

evidence_for.text: BLS did adopt hedonic quality adjustments and geometric-weighting substitution methods starting in the late 1980s-1990s, changes documented in BLS technical notes.
In March 2023, official CPI showed 5% annual inflation while ShadowStats' alternate index showed 14.1%.

evidence_against.text: ShadowStats' methodology does not recompute inflation with historical formulas; it adds an estimated offset to the official BLS figure.
Economists estimate the cumulative effect of BLS methodology changes since the 1980s at a fraction of a percentage point, not several points.

theories.steelman: Cost-of-living-adjusted government payouts (Social Security COLA, TIPS) are pegged to CPI, so any systemic downward bias in the formula — even an unintentional one from substitution/hedonic methods — mechanically saves the government money and benefits bond issuers, a real conflict of interest in who controls the yardstick even absent proof of intentional rigging.

sources.title: Shadowstats.com
No, the real inflation rate isn't 15 percent
Is Inflation 5% Or 14%? It Depends On How It's Measured
Shadowstats debunked

theories.research_note: ShadowStats itself dates to the early 2000s; this record reflects its heavy recirculation during the 2021-2023 inflation surge.

theories.name: Greedflation: Coordinated Corporate Price-Fixing
theories.summary: Holds that large, consolidated industries (groceries, energy, consumer goods) used 2021-2023 supply-chain inflation as cover to coordinate price hikes far beyond their own cost increases, boosting profit margins in what critics call cartel-like behavior. IMF and IPPR/Common Wealth studies found corporate profits, not just costs, drove a large share of 2022 inflation and that margins hit multi-decade highs. Mainstream economists distinguish this from a proven illegal price-fixing conspiracy: concentrated firms can raise prices in parallel without explicit coordination, and no antitrust case has proven cross-company collusion behind the broad 'greedflation' claim, as opposed to sector-specific cases like meatpacking or apartment rents.

evidence_for.text: The IMF found roughly 45% of 2022 eurozone inflation attributable to domestic corporate profits.
A study of 1,350 firms across multiple economies found profits rose 30% from 2019-2022, outpacing inflation.

evidence_against.text: Economists note that oligopoly pricing power raising margins 'under cover' of inflation is parallel pricing behavior, not proven explicit collusion, absent a specific antitrust case.
Mainstream economic analysis finds corporate profit-taking alone does not explain the majority of the 2021-2023 US inflation episode.

theories.steelman: Where concentrated industries share detailed competitor data through common vendors or trade groups — as later proven in the RealPage rent-fixing case and the meatpacking wage-fixing settlements — 'greedflation' becomes a specific, provable coordination mechanism rather than mere parallel pricing; the open question is how many other sectors work the same way.

sources.title: Greedflation
Once a fringe theory, 'greedflation' gets its due
'Greedflation' study finds many companies were lying to you about inflation
Last Word: Busting the 'Greedflation' Myth
Think corporate greed is the leading cause of inflation? Think again

theories.name: Engineered Inflation to Erase the National Debt ("Silent Default")
theories.summary: Argues that the Federal Reserve and Treasury deliberately tolerated or engineered the 2021-2023 inflation surge as a covert strategy to shrink the real value of the $30-plus trillion national debt at the expense of savers, wage earners, and fixed-income holders — a 'silent default' in place of a formal one. The underlying mechanism is real and historically documented: 1946-1955 inflation cut the US debt/GDP ratio by roughly 40%. But no internal document, testimony, or leak has shown the 2021 'transitory inflation' stance, or the 2022-2023 rate hikes that followed and reversed course, were secretly designed for debt reduction rather than pursued for the Fed's stated employment and price-stability mandates.

evidence_for.text: Academic modeling shows sustained 6% inflation over four years can cut the debt/GDP ratio by roughly 20%.
Historical precedent: inflation from 1946-1955 reduced the US debt/GDP ratio by nearly 40% over a decade.

evidence_against.text: The Federal Reserve's 2022-2023 rate hikes were the fastest in four decades, working directly against any 'inflate away the debt' strategy.
No documentary or testimonial evidence has surfaced that debt reduction, rather than the Fed's dual mandate, drove 2021-2023 policy.

theories.steelman: Inflating away debt is a documented historical tool that worked exactly this way after WWII, and the fact that the strategy 'works' regardless of stated intent means officials who tolerate inflation over more painful fiscal tightening are choosing a path with this effect, even without an admitted plan.

sources.title: The National Debt: A Conspiracy of Silence
Is The Fed Trying To Inflate Away US Debt?
Using inflation to erode the US public debt
We Traded a Debt Crisis for an Inflation Crisis, and Now We Have Both

theories.name: The Fed's 'Secret' 2023 Bank Bailout (BTFP)
theories.summary: Claims the Federal Reserve's March 2023 Bank Term Funding Program, created after Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank failed, was structured and marketed to disguise a bailout of the banking system's unrealized bond losses as a routine liquidity facility, and that its true scale — over $130-140 billion drawn weekly by late 2023/early 2024 — was undersold to the public and Congress as a modest emergency tool. The Fed publicly announced and fully documented the BTFP's terms, so it was not secret in the literal sense, but critics across the political spectrum argue the 'not a bailout' framing and below-market collateral terms concealed the true size and duration of the rescue.

evidence_for.text: BTFP let banks post underwater Treasuries/MBS at face value rather than market value, creating an arbitrage that let banks profit by borrowing from the Fed and re-depositing at higher rates — the Fed closed this loophole in January 2024.
Weekly BTFP borrowing hit a record $141 billion in January 2024, ten months after being introduced as an emergency measure.

evidence_against.text: The BTFP was announced in a public Federal Reserve press release the day it was created, with full terms disclosed.
The program had a fixed one-year term and stopped issuing new loans on schedule in March 2024, consistent with a disclosed emergency facility rather than an open-ended hidden one.

theories.steelman: The 2008-era TARP was a named, voted-on 'bailout'; the BTFP achieved a similar function — propping up banks sitting on unrealized losses — through a Fed facility that never used the word 'bailout' and drew far less legislative scrutiny, a legitimate point about how bank rescues get framed to avoid political backlash.

sources.title: Effective Immediately, Fed Shuts Down Arbitrage Opportunity with the BTFP
Federal Reserve's banking bailouts reach a new weekly high of $103B
Federal Reserve Board announces new funding program
Bank Term Funding Program
What did the Fed do after Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank failed?

theories.name: BRICS Currency Will Collapse the Dollar
theories.summary: A viral claim, amplified by gold and crypto commentators and some US politicians, that BRICS nations are on the verge of launching a shared gold-backed currency that will imminently dethrone the US dollar as world reserve currency, collapsing US purchasing power and government finance. Real de-dollarization exists in narrow form — China-Russia and other bilateral trade increasingly settled in local currencies — but no BRICS summit has agreed to a common currency, and the dollar's share of global reserves has declined only gradually over decades rather than collapsing.

evidence_for.text: Russia and China now conduct the large majority of their bilateral trade in yuan and rubles rather than dollars.
BRICS leaders Putin and Lula publicly called for reduced dollar dependence at 2023-2024 summits, and Lula proposed a common BRICS currency in August 2023.

evidence_against.text: No BRICS summit has produced an agreed common currency; analysts call a BRICS currency 'economically impossible' or a 'political fantasy' given members' divergent economies and lack of fiscal union.
The vast majority of global trade and reserves, including among BRICS members, remains dollar-denominated.

sources.title: From De-Risking to De-Dollarisation: The BRICS Currency and the Future of the International Financial Order
Can BRICS Really Drop the Dollar?
The Difficult Realities of the BRICS' Dedollarization Efforts
The BRICS Currency Fantasy: Why the Push to Dethrone the U.S. Dollar is Economically Impossible

theories.name: FedNow Is a Secret CBDC Control Grid
aliases: Fed to ban cash via FedNow
theories.summary: Claims that the Federal Reserve's FedNow instant-payment system, launched in July 2023, either is itself a central bank digital currency or is the covert first step toward one, designed to let the government track, freeze, or restrict individual spending and eliminate cash. FedNow is in fact a bank-to-bank interbank settlement rail functionally similar to preexisting systems like FedACH and Fedwire; the Fed has stated repeatedly it is not a digital currency, is not linked to individual consumer accounts, and does not replace cash, and any actual US CBDC would require new authorizing legislation from Congress.

evidence_for.text: Fed officials and outside researchers have separately studied a possible future US central bank digital currency, so government interest in the broader concept is real.

evidence_against.text: The Federal Reserve issued a public FAQ explicitly stating FedNow is not a CBDC and not a step toward eliminating cash.
FedNow only settles between banks and credit unions, the same function performed by preexisting systems like FedACH; it creates no new individual-level Fed account or surveillance capability.

sources.title: A TikTok video wrongly conflates FedNow with a possible CBDC
Is the FedNow Service replacing cash? Is it a central bank digital currency?
DeSantis and RFK Jr. misconstrue FedNow and digital dollar plans
Federal Reserve's 'FedNow' Launch Triggers Fresh Speculation Over Digital Dollar

theories.name: The Big Three's Common-Ownership Cartel (BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street)
theories.summary: Holds that BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street — collectively the largest shareholder in a plurality of major US public companies — use overlapping ownership stakes in competing firms to coordinate reduced output and higher prices across entire industries, rather than each pursuing shareholder value independently. This moved from an academic antitrust theory to litigation in 2024, when Texas and other states sued the three asset managers alleging they used shared ESG/'Net Zero' initiatives to coordinate a reduction in coal production across competing coal companies; a federal judge allowed the case to proceed past motions to dismiss in 2025, and Vanguard settled in early 2026 without admitting wrongdoing.

evidence_for.text: The Big Three collectively hold the largest stake in roughly 40% of all US public companies.
A federal judge in Texas largely denied BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street's motions to dismiss the states' antitrust suit in August 2025, allowing common-ownership coordination claims to proceed.
Vanguard settled the case for $29.5 million in early 2026, agreeing to bar its US business from joining climate-focused investment coalitions, without admitting wrongdoing.

evidence_against.text: BlackRock and State Street continue to actively contest the case and have not been found liable by any court.
Passive index-fund proxy voting is largely automated and policy-driven rather than the product of company-specific coordination meetings, according to the funds' own disclosures.

theories.steelman: Antitrust economists have published peer-reviewed 'common ownership' research since the 2010s arguing that when the same handful of asset managers hold large stakes in every airline or every bank, those firms have less incentive to compete on price — and the 2024-2026 coal case is the first time a court has let that theory proceed as an anticompetitive-conspiracy claim rather than just an academic hypothesis.

sources.title: FTC and DOJ File Statement of Interest in Energy Collusion Case Against BlackRock, State Street, and Vanguard
BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street bid to dismiss coal antitrust case fails
Attorney General Ken Paxton Sues BlackRock, State Street, and Vanguard
The Biggest Antitrust Story You've Never Heard

theories.name: Plunge Protection Team Secretly Props Up the Stock Market
aliases: Working Group on Financial Markets conspiracy
theories.summary: Claims that the President's Working Group on Financial Markets — nicknamed the 'Plunge Protection Team' since its 1988 creation — does not merely coordinate policy advice but covertly directs banks like Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley to buy futures and stocks during selloffs to engineer market rebounds, including cited 'recoveries' after sharp 2018 and 2020s drops. The Working Group's documented, public mandate is coordination and recommendations among Treasury, Fed, SEC, and CFTC officials during crises; no public record, court filing, or leak has ever shown it executing trades, and sharp reversals attributed to it have documented alternative explanations such as dip-buying and short covering.

evidence_for.text: The Working Group on Financial Markets is a real body, created by Executive Order 12631 in 1988 after the 1987 crash, with a documented mandate to coordinate crisis response.

evidence_against.text: No public evidence — court record, leaked document, or whistleblower account — shows the Working Group has ever directly executed stock or futures trades.
Analysts attribute cited 'PPT rescues,' such as the late-December 2018 rally, to ordinary dip-buying and short covering rather than coordinated intervention.

sources.title: Working Group on Financial Markets
The 'Plunge Protection Team' — fact or fiction?
What Is the 'Plunge Protection Team' and Is It Really Steering the Stock Market?
The Role of the Plunge Protection Team (PPT) in Financial Markets

theories.name: AI Boom Circular Deals Prop Up the Market
theories.summary: Argues that Nvidia, OpenAI, Microsoft, Oracle, and AMD are engaged in a deliberately circular web of investments and purchase commitments — Nvidia investing in OpenAI, which buys Nvidia chips and Microsoft/Oracle cloud capacity, while Microsoft and AMD hold OpenAI-linked stakes — designed to inflate each other's reported revenue and stock prices and mask that AI capital spending vastly outpaces AI revenue, propping up a stock market where AI-related names drove roughly 80% of 2025 US equity gains. The circular financing structure itself is real and documented by Bloomberg and other outlets as a genuine bubble risk; whether it constitutes deliberate, coordinated deception versus companies independently pursuing self-interested but mutually reinforcing deals remains disputed among financial analysts, not proven intent.

evidence_for.text: OpenAI committed to roughly $1.4 trillion in data-center and compute spending through the early 2030s while reporting about $20 billion in annual revenue and projecting continued annual losses through 2028.
Nvidia, Microsoft, Oracle, and AMD each hold financial stakes in or major supply/purchase contracts with OpenAI, creating overlapping related-party transactions among the same small set of firms.
AI-related companies accounted for roughly 80% of total US stock market gains in 2025.

evidence_against.text: Analysts describe the deals as unusual in scale but comparable in kind to dot-com-era vendor financing, not proof of coordinated fraud rather than aggressive but disclosed business strategy.
All cited deals were publicly announced in press releases and SEC filings rather than hidden from investors.

theories.steelman: Even without proven bad intent, related-party circular financing at hundred-billion-dollar scale creates the same incentive problems seen in past bubbles (Enron's related-party vehicles, dot-com vendor financing): each company's reported growth partly depends on the others' spending, so a slowdown by any one participant can cascade — exactly the structural conflict of interest critics point to.

sources.title: AI Circular Deals: How Microsoft, OpenAI and Nvidia Keep Paying Each Other
The AI boom's reliance on circular deals is raising fears of a bubble
Here's why concerns about an AI bubble are bigger than ever
AI bubble

theories.name: Stock Buybacks as Engineered Executive Wealth Transfer
theories.summary: Claims that corporate stock buybacks are deliberately timed and sized against executive stock-based compensation grants to mechanically inflate earnings-per-share metrics that trigger bonus payouts, transferring wealth from long-term shareholders and workers to executives, rather than functioning as the 'returning capital to shareholders' story companies tell publicly. Buybacks' effect of offsetting share dilution from executive stock grants and boosting EPS-linked pay is documented in corporate governance research; whether individual buyback programs are consciously engineered for that specific purpose, versus being one of several disclosed motives boards weigh, is contested.

evidence_for.text: Research on executive compensation finds buybacks reduce share count in ways that mechanically raise EPS figures used in incentive-pay formulas.
Buybacks are estimated to have transferred over a trillion dollars from shareholders to executives and managers over the past decade via stock-based compensation offsets.

evidence_against.text: Buyback proponents note repurchases are one of several legitimate capital-allocation tools and, done at fair value, can increase remaining shareholders' per-share value rather than solely benefiting executives.
Buyback timing and volume are SEC-regulated under Rule 10b-18, and full boards, not individual executives, approve repurchase programs.

sources.title: Share Buybacks and Executive Compensation: Assessing Key Criticisms
The Buyback Divide: How Stock Buybacks Distort Markets and Deepen Wealth Inequality
Stock Buybacks!™ and the Monetization of Stock-Based Compensation
Share Buybacks and Executive Compensation: Assessing Key Criticisms

theories.name: Congressional Insider Trading (the "Pelosi Tracker" Phenomenon)
theories.summary: Claims that members of Congress — Nancy Pelosi and her husband Paul Pelosi have become the emblematic example — systematically trade stocks (or have spouses trade) using non-public information gained through committee work and legislative briefings, producing market-beating returns that a cottage industry of 'Pelosi tracker' apps now lets ordinary investors copy. Documented instances include Paul Pelosi's 2022 Nvidia trades ahead of semiconductor-subsidy legislation and Visa stock sales before a DOJ antitrust suit was announced; Congress members are covered by the 2012 STOCK Act's disclosure and trading rules, but enforcement has been minimal, no insider-trading charge has ever been brought against Pelosi, and multiple studies find congressional trading portfolios outperform the market on average.

evidence_for.text: Paul Pelosi purchased Nvidia shares ahead of 2022 semiconductor legislation Congress was considering, and sold Visa stock months before the DOJ announced an antitrust suit against the company.
Academic studies of congressional stock trading have found average portfolio returns for members of Congress outperform the broader market.

evidence_against.text: No insider-trading charge or conviction has ever been brought against Nancy or Paul Pelosi, and Pelosi has denied any wrongdoing.
All cited trades were legally disclosed under the STOCK Act, meaning outperformance could reflect access to public non-material information or timing skill rather than proven illegal insider trading.
