theories.steelman: Even setting aside any single trade, the structural conflict of interest — lawmakers voting on bills that predictably move stocks they or their spouses hold — is real and is exactly why bipartisan bills, including the 'PELOSI Act' and other proposed stock-trading bans for members of Congress, have been repeatedly introduced since 2023.

sources.title: Pelosi Stock Tracker
Investors, worried they can't beat lawmakers in stock market, copy them instead
Trump Calls Out Pelosi for Latest Insider Trading Controversy
A New Dilemma For Insider Trading: 'PELOSI Act'

theories.name: Citadel-Robinhood Collusion to Kill the GameStop Squeeze
theories.summary: Claims that Robinhood's January 28, 2021 decision to restrict buying of GameStop and other 'meme stocks' was made at the secret behest of Citadel Securities — Robinhood's payment-for-order-flow partner and an investor in the hedge fund Melvin Capital, which held a large short position in GameStop — to protect Citadel/Melvin from further losses rather than for the stated reason of clearinghouse collateral requirements. The SEC's October 2021 report on the episode found the price surge was driven by retail sentiment and buying, criticized payment-for-order-flow incentives generally, but found no evidence of a Robinhood-Citadel conspiracy behind the trading restriction.

evidence_for.text: Citadel Securities was both Robinhood's largest source of payment-for-order-flow revenue and had just recapitalized Melvin Capital, which held a large GameStop short position.
Robinhood's abrupt buy restriction coincided precisely with the peak of the squeeze, fueling widespread retail-investor suspicion of collusion.

evidence_against.text: The SEC's October 2021 report concluded the restrictions were driven by clearinghouse deposit/collateral requirements triggered by extreme volatility, and found no evidence of a conspiracy between Robinhood and Citadel.

Multiple other brokerages independently imposed similar restrictions on the same volatile stocks at the same time, consistent with an industry-wide clearing/margin issue rather than a Robinhood-Citadel-specific plot.

sources.title: The GameStop Episode: What Happened and What Does It Mean?
Debunking the Myth surrounding GameStop: A summary of the SEC investigation
ROBINHOOD/GAMESTOP/CITADEL: MAKING YOUR VOICE HEARD
How the GameStop Hustle Worked

theories.name: Meatpacking Industry Wage-Fixing Cartel
theories.summary: Alleges that major beef and pork processors — Tyson Foods, JBS/Pilgrim's Pride, Cargill, National Beef, and others — illegally shared confidential wage data and entered no-poach agreements to suppress pay for hundreds of thousands of plant workers, and separately coordinated to fix retail prices for beef products, using benchmarking data services to launder the coordination. Unlike most entries in this batch, this is not merely an unproven theory: a related DOJ criminal poultry price-fixing case produced a guilty plea and a $108 million fine, and a $200.2 million class-action wage-fixing settlement with Tyson, JBS, Cargill, National Beef and others received preliminary court approval in January 2025.

evidence_for.text: A $200.2 million class-action settlement (JBS $55M, Tyson $72.5M, Cargill $29.75M, National Beef $14.2M, and others) resolved wage-suppression claims and received preliminary court approval on January 15, 2025.
In the related criminal poultry price-fixing case, Pilgrim's Pride (a JBS unit) pleaded guilty and was sentenced to pay $108 million in fines.
The wage-fixing lawsuits alleged processors shared confidential wage data and made no-poach agreements to suppress plant-worker pay.

evidence_against.text: Defendants in the civil wage-fixing class action settled without admitting legal liability; only the separate criminal poultry price-fixing case produced an admitted guilty plea.

sources.title: $200.2M Settlement With Beef, Pork Processing Plants Ends Class Action Lawsuit Over Alleged Wage Suppression
Tyson Foods, JBS settle wage-fixing lawsuit
DOJ investigating meatpacking companies after Trump price manipulation allegations
Food Price Fixing Is Still Rampant. DOJ's Agri Stats Lawsuit Could Change That.

theories.name: Mass Immigration as Deliberate Wage-Suppression Policy
aliases: Business lobby imports cheap labor to hold down pay
theories.summary: Claims that record levels of migration under the Biden administration (2021-2024) were not simply a border-policy failure but a deliberate, coordinated strategy by government officials and business lobbies to expand the labor supply and suppress wage growth and inflation at the direct expense of native-born and lower-income workers. Economists are genuinely divided on immigration's wage effects — Nobel laureate David Card's research found minimal wage impact on native workers overall, while other economists find measurable effects on the lowest-skilled segment — and since 2022-2023 some economists and advocates have explicitly argued for more immigration as a tool to cool wage-driven inflation, but open advocacy for a policy differs from evidence of secret coordination to suppress wages as its true, hidden purpose.

evidence_for.text: Some economists and advocacy groups who previously argued immigration has little wage impact began explicitly recommending increased immigration in 2022-2023 specifically to ease wage-driven inflation.
Business groups have lobbied publicly and openly for expanded work-visa and immigration levels citing labor shortages, a documented, non-secret form of influence.

evidence_against.text: Nobel Prize-winning research by David Card found immigration does not significantly reduce wages for native-born workers overall.
Advocacy for immigration as anti-inflation policy has been made openly in think-tank reports and public commentary, not through hidden coordination.

sources.title: Open Borders Economists Have Gone Awfully Quiet About Wages
Evidence shows immigration reduces wages significantly
Nobel winner David Card shows immigrants don't reduce the wages of native-born workers
Does Immigration Reduce Wages?

theories.name: RealPage/YieldStar Algorithmic Rent-Fixing
theories.summary: Tenants, state attorneys general, and the DOJ allege that RealPage's YieldStar/AI Revenue Management software let competing landlords controlling roughly 70% of some multifamily markets pool nonpublic pricing, lease, and occupancy data, letting a shared algorithm recommend synchronized rent increases and even advise landlords to hold units vacant rather than compete on price. RealPage and its landlord clients (Greystar, Cortland, and others) deny wrongdoing; RealPage settled with the DOJ in November 2025 without admitting liability, agreeing to a compliance monitor and restrictions on using real-time competitor data.

evidence_for.text: ProPublica's 2022 investigation found RealPage's software ingested competitors' confidential lease and pricing data to generate common rent recommendations across landlords.
DOJ and eight states sued RealPage and major landlords (including Greystar) in August 2024 alleging an antitrust conspiracy covering 16 million units.
The FBI executed an unannounced raid on Blackstone-linked landlord Cortland Management's Atlanta headquarters in May 2024 as part of a criminal antitrust probe.
RealPage's own materials reportedly encouraged landlords to accept higher vacancy in exchange for higher per-unit rents rather than compete for occupancy.
RealPage settled with the DOJ in November 2025, agreeing to a three-year court-monitor and a ban on using real-time nonpublic competitor data in pricing recommendations.

evidence_against.text: The DOJ settlement imposes no fine and RealPage admits no wrongdoing.

State plaintiffs and many landlord defendants have not settled, and the underlying antitrust theory (that using a common vendor's software constitutes a per se price-fixing conspiracy) remains contested in ongoing litigation.

theories.steelman: RealPage's software functioned as a documented clearinghouse for competitively sensitive, nonpublic pricing data among landlords who collectively controlled a large share of local rental supply, which is close kin to how the Libor panel banks used a shared reporting mechanism to align rates — a real settlement, monitor, and criminal probe followed.

sources.title: How a Secret Rent Algorithm Pushes Rents Higher
DOJ Backs Tenants in Price-Fixing Case Against Big Landlords and Real Estate Tech Company
DOJ and RealPage Agree to Settle Rental Price-Fixing Case
FBI raids Cortland office
DOJ Settles Its Algorithmic Price-Fixing Case Against RealPage

theories.research_note: Well-documented via a live DOJ antitrust case, a criminal FBI probe, and a court-filed settlement; scored below 91 because RealPage admits no liability and much litigation remains unresolved.

theories.name: "BlackRock Is Buying All the Houses"
theories.summary: A viral claim, repeated by figures across the political spectrum including Robert F. Kennedy Jr., holds that asset manager BlackRock is secretly buying up single-family homes en masse to lock ordinary Americans out of homeownership and force a "nation of renters." The claim conflates BlackRock (which manages index funds and does not buy individual houses) with Blackstone, a separate private-equity firm whose vehicles (e.g., Invitation Homes) do own tens of thousands of single-family rentals.

evidence_for.text: Blackstone, often confused with BlackRock, did lead large-scale single-family-rental acquisition after 2012 via Invitation Homes and backed a $6B deal for Home Partners of America in 2021.
In March 2026 social media posts claimed BlackRock 'quietly filed' to acquire $22 billion in single-family homes across 14 states, framed as breaking news.

evidence_against.text: BlackRock states directly that buying individual homes is not among its business lines; it manages capital for clients, including index and ETF funds that hold minority public shares in homebuilders and REITs, not direct home ownership.
Snopes found the specific March 2026 '$22 billion across 14 states' filing claim to be baseless and untraceable to any real SEC or regulatory filing.
The Washington Post traced RFK Jr.'s and others' claims to a conflation of BlackRock with Blackstone, two entirely separate companies.

sources.title: BlackRock house-buying conspiracy theory
No, BlackRock Isn't Buying All the Houses
Don't buy claim BlackRock 'quietly filed' to acquire $22B in single-family homes
BlackRock, Blackstone: RFK Jr.'s housing conspiracy theory

theories.research_note: Has its own dedicated Wikipedia article documenting the claim's spread and repeated fact-checks; the underlying premise (BlackRock as a direct home-buyer) is specifically false, distinct from the substantiated Blackstone/Invitation Homes reality.

theories.name: NAR Realtor Commission Cartel (Sitzer/Burnett)
theories.summary: Consumer advocates and antitrust plaintiffs alleged the National Association of Realtors conspired with major brokerages to enforce a rule requiring sellers to pay a fixed ~6% commission split with buyer's agents, artificially inflating costs nationwide. A federal jury in the Sitzer/Burnett trial found in October 2023 that NAR and named brokerages had "knowingly and voluntarily" conspired to inflate commissions, awarding $1.8 billion in damages (trebled to $5.4B); NAR settled in March 2024 for $418 million and eliminated the cooperative-compensation rule.

evidence_for.text: The DOJ investigated NAR's rules restricting MLS access and commission practices as early as 2005.
The October 2023 jury verdict found NAR and defendant brokerages liable for conspiring to inflate broker commission rates via NAR's cooperative-compensation rule.
NAR agreed in March 2024 to pay $418 million over four years and eliminated the rule requiring sellers to set buyer-broker compensation on the MLS.

evidence_against.text: NAR maintains the settlement is not an admission of liability and continues to appeal aspects of related rulings.

sources.title: Burnett v. National Association of Realtors
Jury Finds NAR, Brokerages Guilty in Sitzer Commissions Suit
Judge Approves NAR Settlement in Sitzer/Burnett Case
Moehrl v. National Association of Realtors, et al.

theories.research_note: Court-confirmed: a jury verdict and a $418M settlement place this in the top truth tier alongside other proven price-fixing cartels.

theories.name: Private Equity Engineered Renter Class
theories.summary: Tenant advocates and some commentators claim private-equity firms — especially Blackstone via Invitation Homes — deliberately bought up hundreds of thousands of single-family homes after 2008 and 2020 as part of a knowing, coordinated strategy to permanently convert American homebuyers into renters, sometimes tied to the WEF "you will own nothing" slogan. What is documented is that Blackstone pioneered institutional buy-to-rent at scale starting in 2012 and remains a dominant single-family-rental owner; what is not documented is any coordinated industry-wide plan to eliminate homeownership as opposed to ordinary profit-seeking business expansion.

evidence_for.text: Blackstone founded Invitation Homes in 2012, buying distressed single-family homes at scale in Sunbelt metros like Atlanta, Phoenix, and Orlando, now a public company worth over $18 billion.
The UN special rapporteur on housing criticized Blackstone for worsening the U.S. and global housing crisis through aggressive rent increases and evictions.
Tenant advocacy groups cite corporate statements they characterize as explicit acknowledgment that many buyers are being permanently priced into renting.

evidence_against.text: Institutional investors (all types combined) own a small single-digit percentage of U.S. single-family homes nationally, far short of an ability to unilaterally 'engineer' national tenure patterns.

Blackstone has since 2016 offered some renters preferred-rate paths to buy the homes they rent, with roughly a quarter of exiting renters becoming owners, cutting against a 'trap renters forever' narrative.

Critics note Blackstone's business model responds to post-2008 foreclosure gluts and investor demand for yield, a documented market opportunity rather than a secret plan requiring coordination across the industry.

theories.steelman: Blackstone genuinely pioneered and scaled the buy-to-rent model after the 2008 crash and again in 2020-2021, concentrating ownership in specific metro submarkets enough to draw UN criticism and match the 'renter class' framing rhetorically, even without proof of cross-firm coordination.

sources.title: Here's what happens when private equity buys homes in your neighborhood
U.N. blasts Blackstone Group for worsening the U.S. housing crisis
History and impact of institutional investment in housing in the United States
Blackstone Doesn't Hurt the Housing Market—It Helps It

theories.name: Homebuilder Land-Banking Conspiracy
theories.summary: A long-running claim, recirculated amid the 2021+ housing shortage, holds that large homebuilders deliberately withhold land with planning permission from development to keep housing scarce and prices high, rather than genuinely being constrained by labor, materials, financing, or permitting. Multiple official reviews (UK's Barker 2004, Callcutt 2007, OFT 2008, Lyons 2014) found no evidence of this specific practice, concluding builders' financial incentives favor building and selling as fast as feasible.

evidence_for.text: Proponents point to persistently large 'stock of land with planning permission' figures as circumstantial evidence builders sit on buildable land.

evidence_against.text: The UK Barker Review (2004), Callcutt Review (2007), Office of Fair Trading (2008), and Lyons Review (2014) each independently examined the claim and found no evidence of deliberate withholding to inflate prices.
Homebuilder Federation analysis argues the economics run the opposite way: builders profit by recycling capital into new sites as fast as possible, disincentivizing hoarding.
Much modern 'land banking' is actually third-party land bankers holding lots off builders' balance sheets for capital-efficiency reasons, not builders themselves withholding supply.

sources.title: Land banking by housebuilders: the baseless conspiracy theory that refuses to go away
The land banking myth housebuilders can't shake
Note on house builders and land banking

theories.research_note: This specific claim (builders sitting on permitted land purely to inflate prices) has been repeatedly, formally investigated and rejected; distinct from the more evidence-backed 'consolidation reduces output' critique recorded separately.

theories.name: Homebuilder Cartel / Deliberate Under-Building
theories.summary: Journalist Matt Stoller and allied antitrust commentators argue that consolidation among large public homebuilders (the top 10 builders' market share roughly tripled from 1994 to 2018) has let a smaller number of firms control land and financing and hold vast inventories of empty entitled lots — an estimated 2 million — restricting output to protect prices and shareholder returns, likened to OPEC. Mainstream housing economists dispute that this consolidation, rather than zoning and interest rates, is the primary driver of the shortage, and no antitrust action has found illegal coordination among builders.

evidence_for.text: Top-10 homebuilder market share rose from about 10% in 1994 to nearly a third by 2018, per antitrust analysis.
Large public homebuilders are reported to be sitting on roughly 2 million empty entitled lots even amid a housing shortage.
Large builders reportedly engage in joint ventures with each other on land acquisition, which critics argue can function to hold supply off the market ahead of smaller competitors.

evidence_against.text: NAHB, the industry's own trade group, publicly attributes the housing shortage to regulatory costs, zoning, labor, lumber, and financing constraints ('the Five Ls'), and actively lobbies FOR zoning reform rather than against supply growth.
Economists writing in response to the 'cartel' framing argue zoning restrictions, not builder consolidation, are the dominant constraint on new supply.

sources.title: It's the Land, Stupid: How the Homebuilder Cartel Drives High Housing Prices
Does a Construction Cartel Explain Rising Rents?
5 Key Supply-Side Headwinds Hurt Housing Supply, NAHB Tells Congress
America's housing crisis isn't an accident — it's by design

theories.research_note: Real consolidation and lot-banking data exist and are cited by name by a prominent antitrust journalist, but this is a contested market-structure critique, not a proven illegal-coordination case — scored as plausible/contested rather than confirmed.

theories.name: NIMBY Zoning as Astroturfed Incumbent-Owner/Landlord Lobby
theories.summary: Housing-supply advocates claim that opposition to zoning reform and new development, presented as grassroots neighborhood concern, is actually coordinated or funded by incumbent homeowners and landlord-adjacent interests seeking to protect property values and constrain competing rental supply. Documented lobbying by homeowner associations and real-estate-linked donors to local officials is real; direct evidence of a secretly coordinated 'astroturf' campaign specifically orchestrated by landlords (as opposed to ordinary self-interested homeowner politics) is thin.

evidence_for.text: Academic and advocacy accounts document that NIMBY opposition to affordable and dense housing is disproportionately organized by more affluent, incumbent homeowners with a direct financial interest in restricting new supply near them.
Commentary on grassroots organizing in Los Angeles describes specific instances of manufactured, funded opposition presented as organic neighborhood concern.

evidence_against.text: A competing analysis argues NIMBY activism largely reflects genuine, decentralized homeowner self-interest rather than any organized landlord-funded campaign, and that development opposition varies too much locally to reflect central coordination.

Some commentators invert the astroturf charge entirely, alleging it is the pro-development YIMBY movement that is centrally funded by developer and tech money rather than NIMBYs being landlord-funded.

sources.title: What Is NIMBYism? How Do Affordable Housing Developers Respond to It?
Mow the Lawn! Stop Bad Grassroots Organizing in Los Angeles
Not in Anybody's Backyard: How NIMBY Activism Worsens California's Housing Crisis

theories.research_note: Both directions of the astroturf accusation (NIMBY-funded-by-landlords vs. YIMBY-funded-by-developers) circulate; recorded the direction specified in the research brief.

theories.name: Airbnb/Short-Term-Rental Investors Blamed for Housing Crisis
theories.summary: Politicians (including AOC) and tenant advocates argue Airbnb and the investor-hosts who dominate the platform have knowingly pulled long-term housing, especially starter homes, out of local markets for short-term-rental profit, worsening affordability, while Airbnb lobbies against regulation it knows would return that supply. Airbnb counters that it is a scapegoat for zoning and construction shortfalls, citing cities like Barcelona where prices rose sharply despite falling listing counts.

evidence_for.text: About one-quarter of Airbnb hosts control roughly two-thirds of listings, indicating investor concentration rather than casual home-sharing.
In 2020, vacation-home purchases grew over 40% faster than overall existing-home sales, with buyers explicitly citing short-term-rental income potential.
AOC has publicly linked Airbnb's Puerto Rico expansion to displacement and eviction pressure on long-term residents.

evidence_against.text: Airbnb CEO Brian Chesky notes Barcelona home prices rose 60% over a decade while Airbnb listings there fell, arguing the platform is 'a convenient scapegoat for a failed policy.'

sources.title: 4 Reasons Airbnbs Are Partly to Blame for the Housing Crisis
Is Airbnb Contributing to the Housing Crisis?
AOC takes aim at Airbnb over housing crisis
How Airbnb is Killing Cities

theories.research_note: Recorded as the blame-narrative version of a real, contested debate; the documented facts (investor concentration, lobbying spend) are real, while the 'knowing coordinated harm' framing is the conspiratorial layer.

theories.name: Immigration Policy Deliberately Used to Inflate Housing Demand
theories.summary: Some political figures, notably JD Vance, have claimed that federal immigration policy was deliberately kept loose specifically to inflate housing demand and prices for the benefit of asset-holders, landlords, and homebuilders. Economists broadly agree immigration modestly raises housing demand but find the effect is tiny relative to interest rates and supply constraints, and no evidence supports demand-inflation as a deliberate policy objective rather than a side effect economists actually debate.

evidence_for.text: Economists agree that increased immigration does increase aggregate housing demand to some degree.

evidence_against.text: FactCheck.org and PolitiFact both rated Vance's claims connecting illegal immigration to housing price surges as misleading, noting the magnitude is far smaller than he suggested.
One economist estimated unauthorized immigration's contribution to home price growth at under 1%, roughly 1/100th the impact of higher interest rates.
The National Low Income Housing Coalition's fact sheet with immigrant-family advocacy groups found no evidence for a deliberate demand-inflation policy motive.

sources.title: Vance's Misleading Claims on Housing Prices and Illegal Immigration
PolitiFact | Vice President JD Vance misleads on immigrants' role in US housing crisis
What economists say about JD Vance's claim that immigrants drive up housing costs

theories.research_note: Distinguished from the ethnic-collective 'Great Replacement' theory (already in the atlas, truth 6): this entry records the narrower economic-policy version of the claim about deliberate demand engineering, which is separately unsubstantiated on the evidence of magnitude.

theories.name: Home Insurers Secretly Colluding to Exit High-Risk States
theories.summary: As major insurers pulled back from California, Florida, and other disaster-prone states, some policyholders and consumer advocates allege insurers used state-run 'insurer of last resort' plans like California's FAIR Plan to conceal coordinated price-fixing and coverage restriction, rather than genuinely repricing risk. The broader industry retreat is independently documented as driven by real, large climate-disaster losses and reinsurer reluctance, though specific California litigation (e.g., the Canzoneri/Ferrier cases) alleges insurers used the FAIR Plan pool to disguise collusive rate and coverage decisions.

evidence_for.text: A California lawsuit alleges insurers used the FAIR Plan to conceal collusion, portraying it as a neutral consumer backstop while using it to fix prices and reduce coverage.
Consumer Watchdog has criticized insurers' wildfire catastrophe models as opaque 'black box' algorithms that let companies justify large rate increases without transparency.

evidence_against.text: Insurers lost money on homeowners coverage in 18 states in 2023 alone, and nationwide disaster payouts rose from $30.8B (2013) to $79.6B (2023), consistent with genuine loss-driven repricing rather than manufactured scarcity.
Independent reinsurers (e.g., Swiss Re), who are not part of any alleged primary-insurer collusion, have also become measurably more reluctant to underwrite U.S. catastrophe risk.

sources.title: California Policyholders Say Fire Insurers Colluded to Raise Rates
California's 'last resort' property insurer seeks rate hike, ringing national alarm bells
Climate Risks Present a Significant Threat to the U.S. Insurance and Housing Markets
States beg insurers not to drop climate-threatened homes

theories.name: Insurance AI Claims-Denial Algorithms
theories.summary: Policyholders allege major home insurers (State Farm, Allstate) deploy automated claims-processing and machine-learning tools that systematically flag claims from Black homeowners for extra scrutiny and delay, functioning as a hidden mechanism to reduce payouts while insurers publicly deny discrimination. Lawsuits citing internal data have survived motions to dismiss and a court has allowed discovery specifically into an insurer's AI claims-handling systems.

evidence_for.text: A State Farm lawsuit alleges its claims-processing algorithms disproportionately flag Black policyholders' claims for extra scrutiny, delaying repairs and payments; survey data cited shows white homeowners were nearly a third more likely to have claims processed in under a month.
A court allowed discovery specifically into an insurer's use of AI systems to deny claims, rather than dismissing the theory outright.

Health insurers including Cigna, Humana, and UnitedHealthcare have separately faced lawsuits over 'batch-denial' algorithms alleged to instantly reject thousands of claims without meaningful human review, establishing a documented industry pattern of automated denial tooling.

evidence_against.text: The State Farm lawsuits remain allegations under active litigation; no court has yet issued a final liability finding on the discrimination claims.

theories.steelman: As with Dieselgate, the core allegation is that a company deployed opaque automated software specifically engineered to produce a systematically biased outcome (denials/delays) while publicly presenting its process as neutral — and unlike many finance conspiracy claims, a court has already approved discovery into the algorithm itself rather than dismissing the theory.

sources.title: State Farm hit with lawsuit as policyholders claim AI-driven discrimination
Court Allows Discovery Into Insurer's Use of AI to Deny Claims
Wrongful AI in Claims Handling
State Farm Algorithm Bias Lawsuit

theories.research_note: Stronger evidentiary footing than most entries in this batch: active litigation with a discovery order specifically targeting the algorithm, though no final liability verdict yet exists.

theories.name: Property Tax Assessment Rigging Claims
theories.summary: Claims circulate that property-tax assessors use secret or gamed algorithmic mass-appraisal models to systematically overvalue ordinary homeowners' properties while undervaluing wealthy and commercially connected owners, sometimes framed as a nationwide 'algorithm heist' to hit government budget targets. The strongest real-world basis is Cook County, Illinois, where a ProPublica Illinois investigation ('The Tax Divide') documented Assessor Joseph Berrios's office produced regressive assessments that undervalued expensive properties and overvalued cheaper ones, tied to a system of politically connected tax attorneys; broader nationwide 'secret budget-target algorithm' claims lack comparable documentation.

evidence_for.text: ProPublica Illinois found Cook County's residential assessments under Assessor Berrios were highly regressive, systematically undervaluing higher-priced homes and overvaluing lower-priced ones.
An independent study found the flawed assessments caused a property-tax burden shift exceeding $2 billion, hitting lower-income and minority homeowners hardest.

Commercial and industrial assessments in Cook County were found riddled with errors that undervalued downtown high-value buildings while overvaluing small businesses in poorer neighborhoods, tied to a lucrative practice of politically connected property-tax attorneys.

evidence_against.text: The Cook County scandal is tied to one county's specific mass-appraisal model and one assessor's documented political ties to tax attorneys, not a proven nationwide coordinated scheme.
Broader viral claims of a nationwide 'secret algorithm' rigging home values to a fixed budget target circulate mainly on fringe outlets without comparable documentation or named officials.

sources.title: How the Cook County Assessor failed taxpayers
Flawed Assessments Caused $2 Billion Shift in Property Taxes, Study Finds
Cook County Assessor Faces Questions About Property Tax Inequity
The Property Tax Heist: How Central Appraisal Districts are using secret algorithms to steal your home's equity

theories.research_note: Split score reflects a genuinely documented local scandal (Cook County) lending real evidence to the general grievance, versus an ungrounded fringe-amplified 'nationwide algorithm conspiracy' version with no comparable sourcing.

theories.name: Landlord Vacancy Warehousing Conspiracy
theories.summary: Tenant advocates in New York City allege landlords deliberately keep thousands of rent-stabilized apartments vacant rather than re-rent them at regulated rates, both to pressure lawmakers into rolling back 2019 tenant-protection law and to hold out for future deregulation, rather than because units are genuinely uninhabitable. NYC Comptroller reports confirm a large, sustained rise in vacant-but-unavailable stabilized units and found no evidence that tenant protections (not landlord withholding) caused the vacancies, undercutting industry's stated rationale.

evidence_for.text: A coalition report found roughly 26,000+ rent-stabilized apartments being warehoused by landlords, with the NYC Comptroller finding only about 11.5% too dilapidated to be habitable.

NYC Comptroller Brad Lander's report found the vacancy rate among rent-stabilized units fell to 0.98% by 2023, below the citywide rental vacancy rate, while unavailable-vacant units remained elevated at over 26,000.
Tenant advocates say landlords are holding vacant stabilized units for 'ransom' to lobby against the 2019 Housing Stability and Tenant Protection Act.

evidence_against.text: The NYC Comptroller's report explicitly found no evidence that the 2019 tenant-protection law (HSTPA) caused increased vacancies, countering the landlord industry's own explanation, but that finding cuts against landlords' claimed rationale rather than proving an organized 'cartel'-style withholding scheme.

sources.title: Rent-Stabilized Vacancies Plummeted Over Last 2 Years... New NYC Comptroller Report Finds
More than 60,000 Rent-Stabilized Apartments Are Now Vacant — and Tenant Advocates Say Landlords Are Holding Them for 'Ransom'
New Report Reveals 26,000+ Rent-Stabilized Apartments Being Warehoused by Landlords
Tenant Protections Not to Blame for Vacant Rent-Stabilized Units: NYC Comptroller

theories.research_note: Scoped to NYC where official comptroller data exists; framed as the vacancy-withholding companion to the RealPage algorithmic price-fixing entry, which documents a similar 'accept vacancy over lower rent' logic at the software level.

theories.name: October 7 Deliberate Stand-Down Claim
theories.summary: The claim holds that Israeli political and military leadership had precise foreknowledge of Hamas's October 7 attack plan and deliberately ordered troops to stand down or otherwise allowed the assault to proceed, for domestic political reasons. This goes beyond the documented intelligence failure: Israeli intelligence had obtained a detailed attack blueprint (code-named 'Jericho Wall') over a year in advance and received specific tactical warnings from a Unit 8200 analyst in the days before, but official investigations attribute the failure to threat misassessment and organizational breakdown, not a deliberate order to let the attack happen.

evidence_for.text: The 'Jericho Wall' document, obtained by Israeli intelligence more than a year before the attack, detailed the invasion plan Hamas ultimately executed almost exactly.
A Unit 8200 NCO sent specific warnings in July 2023 and again days before October 7 that Hamas exercises matched the Jericho Wall plan.

evidence_for: Polling after the attack found many Israelis, especially government critics, believed officials knew in advance and failed to act.

evidence_against.text: Israel's Shin Bet internal investigation concluded the core failure was that officials did not consider Hamas's stated battle plan realistic, not that an order was given to permit the attack.
No leaked or declassified order instructing forces to stand down has surfaced despite extensive Israeli and international press investigation.
The claim's most prominent originator, influencer Charlie Kirk, offered no direct evidence beyond speculation about Netanyahu's domestic political troubles.

theories.steelman: Israeli intelligence demonstrably possessed a near-exact attack blueprint and repeated specific tactical warnings that were dismissed rather than acted on, so the claim that Israel was simply 'caught by total surprise' understates real, documented failures of judgment at senior levels — even though no evidence supports an actual order to permit the attack.

sources.title: Denial of the October 7 attacks
Conspiracy Theorists Go Viral With Unsubstantiated Claim About Israel-Hamas Conflict
What went wrong? Israel's spy agency lists failures in preventing Oct. 7 attack
The October 7 Attack: An Assessment of the Intelligence Failings

theories.research_note: The documented intelligence failure (Jericho Wall, ignored warnings) is real and well-sourced; the leap to a deliberate 'stand-down order' is the fringe part being scored.

theories.name: Qatari Cash to Hamas as Netanyahu's Divide-and-Rule Strategy
theories.summary: The claim is that Netanyahu's governments deliberately authorized and encouraged Qatari cash transfers (roughly $15 million a month, delivered in suitcases through Israeli-controlled crossings starting in 2018) to keep Hamas afloat as a rival power center to the Palestinian Authority, specifically to prevent the emergence of a unified Palestinian negotiating partner and a two-state outcome. This is a substantially documented policy, though the claim that Netanyahu personally engineered it as a covert strategic masterplan (rather than a contested, semi-public policy debated within his own government) goes further than the record supports.

evidence_for.text: Qatar sent an estimated $1.8 billion to Gaza since 2012, including monthly cash-filled-suitcase transfers coordinated with and permitted through Israeli territory from 2018 onward.

Former senior Israeli defense official Amos Gilad said the policy of tolerating Qatari transfers was Netanyahu-backed, and was believed within Israeli leadership to weaken Palestinian sovereignty and prospects for statehood.
Reporting indicates Israel asked Qatar to increase transferred funds to Hamas about a month before the October 7 attack.

evidence_against.text: Netanyahu and his office deny seeking to boost Hamas and describe the funding as intended for humanitarian purposes under Israeli security monitoring.
No document has surfaced showing a written Israeli strategic directive to 'build up Hamas'; the policy was contested within Israel's own intelligence community, not a unanimous covert plan.

sources.title: Qatar sent millions to Gaza for years – with Israel's backing
Netanyahu: 'We allowed Qatar to fund Hamas to keep the Palestinians divided'
Did Netanyahu Directly Fund Hamas Through Qatar?
For years, Netanyahu propped up Hamas. Now it's blown up in our faces

theories.name: October 7 Staged Massacre / Crisis Actors Denial
theories.summary: The claim asserts that graphic footage and photos from the October 7 attack were staged or faked using crisis actors, fake blood, and film crews to manufacture international sympathy for Israel and justify the subsequent war. Extensive forensic and journalistic verification, including cross-referenced hostage videos, morgue and hospital records, and on-the-ground reporting, has confirmed the mass-casualty event; the specific viral 'proof' clips cited by proponents have been traced to unrelated footage.
evidence_for: A viral TikTok video showing a film crew and an actor lying on the ground was circulated as proof of staged victims.
Some social media users argued that blood shown in graphic photos looked artificial.

evidence_against.text: Snopes identified the viral 'crisis actor' TikTok video as behind-the-scenes footage from an unrelated April 2022 short film, filmed a year before the attack.
Israeli watchdog CyberWell found roughly 135 posts denying the attack had been viewed by more than 15 million users, part of a documented denial campaign rather than evidence of an actual hoax.
Forensic analysis by outside investigators and international media corroborated large-scale civilian killings using verified footage, morgue records, and witness testimony.

sources.title: Denial and Distortion of the Hamas-led October 7 Attack: An Overview of False Narratives
Denial of the Oct. 7 Hamas attack on Israel is spreading
Denial of the October 7 attacks
Mis- and disinformation and conspiracy theories about the October 7 Hamas attack

theories.name: Gaza Marine Gas Field as Hidden War Motive
theories.summary: The claim holds that Israel's 2023-2025 Gaza campaign is substantially motivated by a desire to seize the offshore Gaza Marine gas field, estimated at 1.1 trillion cubic feet, rather than by the stated security rationale of destroying Hamas after October 7. Analysts note Gaza Marine's reserves are tiny compared to Israel's own Leviathan and Tamar fields, making the economic motive marginal, though Israel had in fact long blocked the field's development out of concern that revenue would benefit Hamas.

evidence_for.text: British Gas estimated Gaza Marine's reserves at 1.1 trillion cubic feet as early as 1999.
Israel blocked Gaza Marine's development for over two decades, reversing course only in June 2023 to give preliminary consent to a Palestinian Authority-Egyptian consortium deal.

evidence_against.text: Gaza Marine's roughly 32 billion cubic meters is small next to Israel's Leviathan (623 bcm) and Tamar (200 bcm) fields, so seizing it would not materially affect Israel's energy independence or export plans.
The war's proximate and stated cause was the October 7 Hamas attack, and Israel's declared war aims center on security and hostage recovery rather than energy assets.

sources.title: The Gaza Oil Myth
The war on Gaza is also an Israeli drive to seize Palestinian gas reserves
Between tales and facts: The long saga of Gaza Marine
Gas at stake in Gaza? Why this theory doesn't hold true

theories.name: Netanyahu Prolonged the War for Political and Legal Survival
theories.summary: The claim is that Netanyahu deliberately extended the Gaza war and blocked ceasefire and normalization deals to delay his corruption trial, avoid early elections he was likely to lose, and prevent the collapse of his far-right coalition. A six-month, 110-interview New York Times investigation found specific decision points supporting this account, including rejecting a unity-government offer and withdrawing support for a hostage-release truce after a coalition partner's threat; Netanyahu's office calls the reporting defamatory and politically motivated.

evidence_for.text: A New York Times investigation based on over 110 interviews concluded Netanyahu shelved a ceasefire plan and obstructed a Saudi normalization deal to avoid risking his right-wing coalition.
The Times reported Netanyahu withdrew support for a six-week truce that would have freed 30 hostages after Finance Minister Smotrich threatened to collapse the government.
Netanyahu has repeatedly sought to delay his own corruption-trial testimony citing wartime demands.

evidence_against.text: Netanyahu's office called the New York Times report 'long discredited' and defamatory toward Israel, its soldiers, and its prime minister.

Israeli military leadership has independently cited unmet security objectives (destroying Hamas's military capacity, hostage recovery) as the basis for continued operations, separate from any political calculation.

theories.steelman: A detailed, multiply-sourced investigative reconstruction of specific wartime cabinet decisions — not just motive-speculation — shows a repeated pattern where ceasefire momentum coincided with coalition-survival threats and Netanyahu chose the option that kept him in power, which is more than pattern-matching even though his office disputes the characterization.

sources.title: Survival over state: NYT probe alleges Netanyahu prolonged Gaza war for political gain
NY Times rejects Netanyahu's denial of report he prolonged Gaza war to stay in power
Netanyahu Criminal Trial Will Resume After Iran Ceasefire—But He Wants To Delay Testimony
A Calculus of Conflict: Netanyahu's Political Survival Through Extended War

theories.name: Ben-Gurion Canal as Hidden Gaza War Motive
theories.summary: The claim holds that Gaza's depopulation and destruction is being carried out to clear the route for a revived 'Ben-Gurion Canal,' a sea-to-sea shipping route that would rival the Suez Canal, undercut Egypt, and give Israel control over a major global trade artery. The original 1960s canal concept, declassified decades later, ran through Israel's Negev far south of Gaza; no current Israeli government plan proposes routing any canal through the Gaza Strip itself.

evidence_for.text: A 1963 US feasibility study for a Negev-route sea-to-sea canal existed and remained classified for roughly 30 years.

evidence_for: A canal rivaling Suez would carry major strategic and economic value, giving proponents a plausible-sounding motive to attach to the war.

evidence_against.text: The original 1960s proposal routed the canal through the Negev via Eilat and the Jordanian border, not through the Gaza Strip, which the modern viral claim asserts.
No Israeli government document, budget line, or infrastructure plan from the current war period proposes constructing a canal through Gaza.

sources.title: What is Israel's Ben Gurion canal plan and why Gaza matters
The 'Ben Gurion Canal': A new crazy anti-Israeli conspiracy theory
How is the Proposed Ben Gurion Canal Tied to Israel's Gaza Invasion?
Gaza's genocide, the Ben-Gurion canal, and the politics of reconstruction

theories.research_note: Sourcing on the pro-theory side is dominated by advocacy outlets and a viral TikTok origin; the main rebuttal piece is itself an opinion/advocacy publication, so treat both directions as contested-quality sourcing.

theories.name: Gaza Humanitarian Foundation Sites as Designed Kill/Displacement Traps
aliases: Aid Sites as Ethnic Cleansing Tool
theories.summary: The claim holds that the US- and Israeli-backed Gaza Humanitarian Foundation's aid distribution sites, where Israeli forces and GHF-hired contractors killed over 2,600 Palestinians seeking food between May and October 2025, were deliberately designed to kill and forcibly displace civilians under cover of humanitarian aid, rather than being a well-intentioned system undone by chaos and rules-of-engagement failures. UN officials and Doctors Without Borders have publicly used language like 'orchestrated killing' and a 'fig leaf for further violence and displacement,' though US and Israeli officials attribute the deaths to crowd-control failures rather than an intentional kill design.

evidence_for.text: Doctors Without Borders described the pattern of mass casualties at GHF sites as 'orchestrated killing' rather than incidental accidents, based on clinic staff accounts.
UN aid chief Tom Fletcher called the GHF system a 'fig leaf for further violence and displacement' of Palestinians.
More than 2,600 Palestinian civilians were killed and thousands wounded seeking aid at or near GHF sites between May 27 and October 9, 2025.

evidence_against.text: US and Israeli officials attribute the repeated shootings to crowd-control and rules-of-engagement failures at chaotic, poorly designed choke points, not an intentional plan to kill civilians.
GHF disputes the 'designed to kill' characterization and states it delivered large volumes of aid despite the security incidents.

theories.steelman: The scale and repetition of mass-casualty incidents at a small number of predictable choke points, combined with reported internal warnings about displacement risk before launch, make 'foreseeable and tolerated' a more defensible reading than pure accident, even short of proving top-down intent to kill.

sources.title: Gaza Humanitarian Foundation
US-backed aid distribution points in Gaza are sites of orchestrated killing
U.N. agency slams U.S.-backed Gaza aid effort as 'a death trap'
2025 Gaza Strip aid distribution killings

theories.name: Lavender AI Kill-List System and Its Cover-Up
theories.summary: The claim, based on interviews with six named Israeli military intelligence officers, is that the IDF built and relied on an AI system called 'Lavender' to mass-generate bombing targets (an estimated 37,000 people in the war's early weeks), that officers gave the system's output near-automatic approval with human review sometimes lasting only about 20 seconds per target, and that the military accepted a roughly 10% known error rate — details which Israeli officials have downplayed publicly. The core reporting comes from investigative journalists at +972 Magazine and Local Call, and has been corroborated in academic and legal analysis, though specific casualty-ratio figures rest on anonymous sources.

evidence_for.text: Six Israeli intelligence officers described Lavender flagging as many as 37,000 Palestinians as suspected militants for potential bombing during the war's early stages.
Officers said human personnel often served as a 'rubber stamp,' spending around 20 seconds per target before authorizing a strike.
UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres said he was 'deeply troubled' by reports of Israel's AI-assisted targeting practices.

evidence_against.text: The IDF has publicly defended aspects of its AI-assisted targeting process as compliant with proportionality review and disputed the 'rubber stamp' characterization of human oversight.
Specific casualty-ratio and error-rate figures rely on unnamed intelligence sources rather than independently verified official documents.

sources.title: 'Lavender': The AI machine directing Israel's bombing spree in Gaza
AI-assisted targeting in the Gaza Strip
Lavender: Israel offers glimpse into terrifying world of military AI
The Gospel, Lavender, and the Law of Armed Conflict

theories.research_note: This is unusually well-sourced investigative journalism corroborated by multiple named/unnamed military insiders and legal scholarship, closer to a substantiated scandal than a fringe theory; the 'cover-up' framing applies to Israel's public downplaying of the reported details, not to the existence of the program.

theories.name: STOIC Covert Israeli Influence Campaign on US Lawmakers
theories.summary: This is a documented covert influence operation: Israel's Ministry of Diaspora Affairs paid the Tel Aviv political consulting firm STOIC roughly $2 million to run a network of fake social-media personas (posing as US students, Black Americans, and 'concerned citizens') using AI-generated content to lobby members of Congress, especially Democrats, to continue supporting Israel after October 7. The operation was uncovered by watchdog group FakeReporter and reported by the New York Times, and OpenAI and Meta both confirmed removing accounts and content tied to it.

evidence_for.text: FakeReporter identified five websites tied to STOIC, an Israeli firm paid about $2 million by Israel's Ministry of Diaspora Affairs to run the campaign.
OpenAI confirmed STOIC used its tools to generate articles and comments distributed by the fake accounts.
Meta suspended or removed 510 Facebook accounts and 32 Instagram accounts linked to the campaign.

evidence_against.text: Researchers found the campaign's actual persuasive effect on Congress members' voting behavior was likely minimal, with most fake accounts having very small followings.

evidence_against: The operation is openly documented and its funding acknowledged after exposure, so it functions more as a confirmed scandal than a hidden theory in dispute.

sources.title: Israeli influence campaign covertly targets U.S. lawmakers
A covert Israeli online influence campaign tried to sway American lawmakers
Israeli influence operation highlights global disinformation industry
Israel's secret influencer campaign exposed

theories.name: TikTok Ban Driven by Israel-Related Content Concerns
theories.summary: The claim is that the 2024 US law forcing TikTok's divestiture or ban was substantially driven by pro-Israel actors, including AIPAC, seeking to suppress pro-Palestinian content, rather than by the stated national-security concerns about Chinese ownership. A leaked Israeli Foreign Ministry memo blaming TikTok's algorithm for turning youth against Israel lends some support to an Israel-related motive among advocates, but investigators found no evidence AIPAC drafted or specifically lobbied for the bill, and the legislation's China-focused rationale and momentum predate October 7 by years.

evidence_for.text: An internal Israeli Foreign Ministry memo, reported by NPR, blamed TikTok's algorithm for 'turning young people against Israel.'

evidence_for: The stalled TikTok divestiture bill regained legislative momentum in Congress in the months following the October 7 attack and rising pro-Palestinian sentiment among young TikTok users.

evidence_against.text: There is no evidence AIPAC was involved in drafting the bill, and its congressional summit agenda around the time did not include the TikTok legislation.
The ADL identified the 'Israel did this' narrative itself as a conspiracy theory, noting it has also been promoted by Iranian state media.

sources.title: TikTok Ban Feared, Antisemitic Conspiracy Theories Follow
Tehran cooked up a conspiracy theory blaming Israel for US TikTok ban
Congress Accused of Using TikTok Ban to Silence Israel Criticism
'This Is AIPAC at Work': Landmark TikTok Vote in U.S. House Provokes Conspiracy Theory

theories.name: Canary Mission as Covert Israeli-Government Deportation Tool
theories.summary: The claim is that Canary Mission, an anonymously run website since 2014 that publishes dossiers on pro-Palestinian students and professors, is not merely a privately funded advocacy blacklist but is covertly coordinated with the Israeli government and functions as an operational targeting tool for US immigration enforcement. A DHS official testified to a 'tiger team' assembled to identify pro-Palestinian noncitizen students for removal, and Canary Mission publicly claimed credit for triggering the detention of Tufts student Rumeysa Ozturk, but no leaked document has shown a formal Israeli-government tasking relationship with the site.

evidence_for.text: A DHS official testified in court that the department assembled a specialized 'tiger team' to identify pro-Palestinian college students for removal.
Canary Mission publicly took credit for its dossier being the primary cause of Tufts graduate student Rumeysa Ozturk's detention by masked immigration officers.
The site's operators and core funding sources have remained anonymous since 2014 despite years of journalistic attempts at disclosure.

evidence_against.text: Identified funders disclosed in tax filings are private US charitable foundations (e.g. the Peisach and Fromer family foundations), not the Israeli state.

evidence_against: No declassified or leaked document establishes a formal tasking or operational relationship between Canary Mission and any Israeli government body; DHS has not confirmed relying specifically on Canary Mission dossiers.

sources.title: Who Is Funding Canary Mission? Inside the Doxxing Operation Targeting Anti-Zionist Students and Professors
Canary Mission: How US uses a 'hate group' to target Palestine advocates
Canary Mission
Canary Mission's Newest Funders

theories.research_note: The doxxing operation and DHS 'tiger team' are independently documented facts; the disputed conspiratorial element is specifically whether Canary Mission operates under Israeli government direction rather than as an independently funded private blacklist.

theories.name: Billionaire Network Secretly Coordinated Campus Protest Crackdown
theories.summary: This is a largely documented claim: a group of pro-Israel business leaders, including Howard Schultz, Michael Dell, Bill Ackman, Joshua Kushner, Daniel Loeb, and Len Blavatnik, privately coordinated over chat and a Zoom call with New York City Mayor Eric Adams in April 2024 to press for a police crackdown on the Columbia University pro-Palestinian encampment, including offering to fund private investigators to assist. Leaked WhatsApp messages, obtained and published by the Washington Post, form the core evidence; the city has denied actually deploying privately funded investigators.

evidence_for.text: The Washington Post published leaked WhatsApp chat logs showing the group coordinated a video call with Mayor Adams on April 26, 2024 covering donations, pressuring Columbia's president, and funding investigative efforts.
Some members of the group offered to pay for private investigators to assist NYPD in handling the protests, according to the chat log, and reported that Adams accepted the offer.

evidence_against.text: New York City officially stated it has not used and is not using privately funded private investigators.

evidence_against: It remains unclear how much the group's pressure actually changed the police response versus what city officials would have done independently given the encampment's scale.

sources.title: Pro-Israel billionaires urged New York crackdown on Gaza protests: Report
Business leaders pressed NYC Mayor Adams to disperse Columbia protesters: report
Reporting on Influence of Pro-Israel Funders Is Not Antisemitic

theories.name: Soros-Funded Tents Astroturfing Campus Encampments
theories.summary: The claim, promoted by NYC Mayor Eric Adams and Rep. Virginia Foxx among others, is that pro-Palestinian campus encampments in 2024 were not organic student organizing but were secretly funded and orchestrated by George Soros or similar dark-money networks, with identical-looking tents cited as proof of centralized supply. Investigators traced the matching tents to bulk retailer discounts on popular camping brands rather than any coordinated procurement, and researchers found the protests were organized by ordinary student coalitions.
evidence_for: Many encampment tents across different university campuses were visibly the same brand and color.

evidence_for.text: George Soros's Open Society Foundations and the Rockefeller Brothers Fund have given grants to Jewish Voice for Peace, a group involved in some protest organizing.

evidence_against.text: The matching tents are explained by online retailer discounts and promotions on a small number of popular camping-brand products, not coordinated dark-money procurement.
Researchers who studied the protests found they were organized and staffed by ordinary college students, not outside operatives.

sources.title: 2024 pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses
What the Media Get Wrong About Campus Protests
Crowd Counting Consortium: An Empirical Overview of Recent Pro-Palestine Protests at U.S. Schools

theories.name: Pallywood: Palestinians Staging Their Own Casualties
theories.summary: Coined in 2005 by Richard Landes and massively revived after October 2023, the claim is that Palestinians and sympathetic media routinely stage or fake scenes of death and injury using 'crisis actors' to manufacture international sympathy, and that this pattern should be used to discount video evidence of Israeli strikes on civilians. Fact-checkers at BBC Verify and Logically Facts traced the viral 'proof' clips used to support the theory to misattributed or miscaptioned footage of real, unrelated victims.
evidence_for: Proponents circulate side-by-side photo comparisons claiming the same 'crisis actor' appears in different casualty photos.

evidence_for.text: Richard Landes's original 2005 video cited specific incidents he argued showed staged reenactments by Palestinian camera crews.

evidence_against.text: BBC Verify found usage of the term 'Pallywood' peaked at 220,000 uses on Twitter/X in November 2023, tracking a coordinated disinformation push rather than an actual pattern of staging.
Widely circulated 'proof' photos comparing two supposedly identical crisis actors were shown to depict two different real people, including one who lost a leg in an actual Israeli military raid.

sources.title: Pallywood
Propaganda Campaigners Use 'Pallywood' Label to Accuse Palestinians of Staging Civilian Casualties
'Pallywood propaganda': Pro-Israeli accounts online accuse Palestinians of staging their suffering
What Is Pallywood? Palestinians Are Falsely Accused of Faking Carnage

theories.name: Hamas as an Israeli-Created or Controlled Asset
theories.summary: The strong version of this claim, revived after October 7 by figures including Alex Jones, holds that Israel deliberately created Hamas from scratch and continues to covertly control its operations as a strategic tool. This overstates a genuine, documented history: Israel tolerated and indirectly supported Sheikh Ahmed Yassin's Islamist charity network through the 1970s and 1980s as a counterweight to the secular PLO, a policy that ended once that network reorganized into Hamas and began attacking Israelis in 1989; no evidence shows Israel founded Hamas or currently directs its military operations, which have repeatedly killed Israelis including on October 7.

evidence_for.text: Israel provided support to Ahmed Yassin's network of Islamic schools, mosques, and clubs through the 1970s and into the First Intifada, viewing it as a useful counterweight to the PLO.
Far-right Israeli finance minister Bezalel Smotrich has publicly called Hamas an 'asset' compared to the Palestinian Authority's 'burden.'

evidence_against.text: Hamas was founded independently in 1987 by Yassin as a distinct Muslim Brotherhood offshoot, not manufactured or staffed by Israeli intelligence.
Israeli tolerance of the Yassin network ended once Hamas began launching attacks on Israelis in 1989, and no document shows continuing Israeli command control over Hamas's military wing.

sources.title: Israeli support for Hamas
Debunking the lie that 'Israel created Hamas'
Why Netanyahu Bolstered Hamas
The myth that Israel/Netanyahu created/funded Hamas
Conspiracy theorist Alex Jones claims 'Netanyahu runs Hamas'

theories.name: Greater Israel: Nile-to-Euphrates Expansion Plan
theories.summary: The claim, revived by Iranian and Hamas officials during the 2023-2025 war, is that the Israeli state secretly pursues, as actual official policy, a biblically-derived plan to expand its borders from the Nile to the Euphrates, citing the Israeli flag's stripes, an old coin design, and an alleged Knesset map inscription as supposed documentary proof. Israeli historians and the national mint have traced each specific piece of 'evidence' to unrelated origins, and no Israeli government platform or coalition agreement has ever adopted Nile-to-Euphrates borders as policy; a fringe minority of religious-nationalist Israelis do invoke the underlying biblical text.
evidence_for: Genesis 15:18 describes a covenant promising Abram's descendants land 'from the river of Egypt unto the great river, the river Euphrates,' a text some fringe religious-nationalist Israelis do cite.
The claim has been repeated by multiple foreign heads of state and Hamas officials as though it were established fact, giving it wide circulation regardless of accuracy.

evidence_against.text: Israel's mint has stated the blue stripes on the Israeli flag derive from the traditional Jewish prayer shawl (tallit), not the Nile and Euphrates rivers.
The claim that a map or inscription over the Knesset entrance depicts Nile-to-Euphrates borders has never been documented photographically and traces to Yasser Arafat-era propaganda.

evidence_against: No Israeli government coalition agreement, party platform, or map has ever formally adopted Nile-to-Euphrates borders as state policy.

sources.title: Greater Israel
Imperial Israel: The Nile-to-Euphrates Calumny
Greater Israel
Inside 'Greater Israel': myths and truths behind the long-time Zionist fantasy

theories.research_note: This differs from ZOG in kind: ZOG claims a hidden ethnic cabal secretly runs foreign governments, while this claim is about an alleged territorial-expansion policy attributed to the Israeli state itself, propped up by specific (and individually debunked) physical 'evidence' claims.

theories.name: AIPAC's Covert Control of Congress via Laundered Donor Money
theories.summary: The claim is that AIPAC's United Democracy Project super PAC functions to covertly launder Republican megadonor money into Democratic primaries specifically to purge members insufficiently supportive of Israel, amounting in practice to disproportionate, hidden control over congressional votes on Middle East policy rather than ordinary transparent lobbying. AIPAC's roughly $100 million in disclosed 2023-24 spending, including a record $14.5 million combined spend defeating Rep. Jamaal Bowman, is real and publicly filed; the 'covert'/'laundering' framing describes legal, disclosed PAC activity that critics argue is misleadingly labeled 'pro-Israel' in press coverage rather than 'Republican-funded.'

evidence_for.text: AIPAC spent approximately $100 million in the 2023-24 election cycle, much of it raised from Republican megadonors, targeting Democrats it viewed as insufficiently supportive of Israel.
The primary race in which AIPAC's United Democracy Project spent roughly $14.5 million to defeat Rep. Jamaal Bowman became the most expensive US House primary in history.
AIPAC's PAC also spent millions opposing Rep. Cori Bush's reelection while separately funding her primary opponent.

evidence_against: All of AIPAC's spending is publicly disclosed under FEC campaign-finance rules, which distinguishes it from a genuinely covert operation even if donors' partisan origin is under-reported in headlines.

AIPAC uses the same legal single-issue-PAC tactics as many other lobbies (e.g. crypto or pharmaceutical PACs), and targeted members remained free to criticize Israel policy up to the moment of their primary defeat, showing the 'control' is electoral pressure rather than direct command over votes.

sources.title: Anti-AIPAC account's co-founder is former staffer for AOC, Bush, Bowman
Bowman crushed by GOP-fueled AIPAC cash
Progressive Jamaal Bowman Loses Seat After Pro-Israel Lobby Boosted Opponent
How AIPAC's spending strategy evolved

theories.research_note: Distinguish carefully from the ZOG trope (already in the atlas at truth 1): this record scores AIPAC specifically, a named, disclosed-spending PAC, not a claim about hidden ethnic control of government in general.

theories.name: Iran Strike 'Obliteration' Cover-Up
theories.summary: Claims that the Trump administration knew the June 2025 strikes on Fordow, Natanz, and Isfahan did not 'completely and totally obliterated' Iran's nuclear program as publicly declared, and moved to discredit and hunt down the leaker rather than release the underlying data once a Defense Intelligence Agency battle-damage assessment showing only a months-long setback (with centrifuges largely intact and enriched uranium reportedly relocated beforehand) became public. A later Pentagon assessment cited a longer one-to-two-year setback, but no fully reconciled public damage accounting has been released.

evidence_for.text: A leaked preliminary DIA Battle Damage Assessment found Iran's program set back only a few months, with some centrifuges intact and Fordow's/Isfahan's lower structures assessed as not destroyed despite caved-in entrances.
Intelligence reportedly shared with European governments indicated Iran's enriched uranium stockpile remained largely intact, since it had been moved prior to the strikes.

White House press secretary called the leak 'a clear attempt to demean President Trump' rather than addressing the substance of the assessment, and an FBI leak investigation was opened instead of releasing full findings.

IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi said damage was difficult to fully evaluate from satellite imagery alone, while confirming centrifuges at Fordow were 'no longer operational' — a narrower claim than 'obliterated.'

evidence_against.text: Defense Secretary Hegseth said the leaked report was explicitly 'preliminary' and 'low confidence,' the normal caveat for early battle-damage assessments before all intelligence streams are integrated.
A subsequent July 2025 Pentagon assessment concluded the program was set back by roughly two years, materially higher than the initial leaked estimate.
CIA Director John Ratcliffe stated new intelligence found the sites 'severely damaged' and would take years to rebuild, directly countering the leaked DIA numbers.

theories.steelman: A real, internally produced document assessing a months-long setback directly contradicted a superlative public claim of total destruction, and the administration's first move was to pursue the leaker rather than publish a full, reconciled damage accounting — a pattern consistent with prior instances where battle-damage claims outran the classified record.

sources.title: Exclusive: Early US intel assessment suggests strikes on Iran did not destroy nuclear sites
U.S. damage report: Iran nuclear program set back by months, not obliterated
Disruption or Dismantlement: Diverging Assessments of Iran Nuclear Strikes
2025 United States strikes on Iranian nuclear sites

theories.research_note: The underlying leak and dispute are well documented by mainstream outlets; the 'cover-up' framing (deliberate suppression vs. normal intelligence-cycle revision) is the contested conspiratorial layer.

theories.name: Manufactured Iran Nuclear Threat
theories.summary: Claims that the US and Israeli governments exaggerated or manufactured intelligence that Iran was on the imminent verge of building a nuclear weapon in order to justify the June 2025 strikes, noting that Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard had testified in March 2025 that the intelligence community assessed Iran was not actively building a weapon — an assessment reversed only weeks before the strikes without full public disclosure of the supposedly new evidence. IAEA inspectors likewise reported advancing enrichment capability but no confirmed active weaponization program.

evidence_for.text: DNI Tulsi Gabbard testified to Congress in March 2025 that the IC assessed Iran was not currently building a nuclear weapon, a position reversed in official messaging by June.
IAEA statements emphasized uncertainty about weaponization intent even while confirming enrichment levels and site damage, a narrower claim than the 'imminent nuclear weapon' framing used publicly.
Independent fact-checks of Trump's public statements justifying the strikes found repeated exaggerations regarding Iran's weapons timeline.

evidence_against.text: Iran's enrichment to 60% purity has no plausible civilian justification and had been repeatedly flagged by IAEA inspectors as inconsistent with peaceful use.

Officials stated new intelligence streams emerged between March and June 2025 that updated the assessment; intelligence reassessment on a fast-moving program is not inherently evidence of fabrication.

sources.title: Intelligence Implications of the Shifting Iran Strike Narrative
Fact-checking statements made by Trump to justify U.S. strikes on Iran
FactChecking Trump's Prime-Time Address on Iran
Trump's Chaotic and Reckless Iran Nuclear Policy

theories.name: Netanyahu's Iran War as Trial Distraction
theories.summary: Claims that Netanyahu pushed for and prolonged the 2025–2026 conflicts with Iran, Hamas, and Hezbollah substantially to delay his own corruption trial and preserve his political survival, rather than purely for security reasons. Proponents point to his corruption trial's proceedings pausing during active conflict and his requests to delay testimony once a ceasefire took hold; Iranian officials made the same accusation publicly. Netanyahu denies any such motive and cites documented security threats as the sole driver.

evidence_for.text: NYT columnist Thomas Friedman wrote that 'keeping Israel at war with Iran, Hamas and Hezbollah enables Netanyahu to drag out his corruption trial.'
Netanyahu's criminal trial, covering bribery and fraud charges dating to 2019, was set to resume after the Iran ceasefire, and he requested a delay to his own testimony.
Iranian Foreign Minister Araghchi publicly speculated Israel and the US were drawing out the conflict specifically to keep Netanyahu out of court.

evidence_against.text: Netanyahu faces documented, independently substantiated national security threats from Iran's nuclear and missile programs that predate his indictment by years.
No direct evidence (memos, testimony) has surfaced showing war timing was coordinated with trial scheduling rather than battlefield conditions.

sources.title: 'The World Sees You, Netanyahu': Israel's Iran Strikes Seen as 'War of Distraction'
Netanyahu Criminal Trial Will Resume After Iran Ceasefire—But He Wants To Delay Testimony
How the Iran war united, and then divided, Trump and Israel's Netanyahu

theories.name: Hidden Iran Regime-Change War Plan
theories.summary: Claims that emerged after the June 2025 strikes, when officials publicly insisted the goal was narrowly counter-proliferation, that the US and Israel actually possessed a far broader covert plan for regime change in Iran involving leadership decapitation and fomenting a popular uprising. Reporting later confirmed such a plan was presented to and adopted by Netanyahu, briefed to senior Trump officials by January 2026, and substantially executed when the US and Israel launched the February 28, 2026 war, which included the killing of Ali Khamenei and explicit regime-change calls to Iranian civilians.

evidence_for.text: A regime-change plan involving galvanizing Iranian opposition through strikes and assassinations to trigger government collapse was presented to Netanyahu and adopted, then briefed to senior Trump officials in mid-January 2026.

Following the February 28, 2026 strikes and the assassination of Ali Khamenei, both Trump and Netanyahu publicly called on Iranian civilians to seize control once government institutions were crippled.

evidence_against.text: During the June 2025 strikes, top officials repeatedly denied regime change was a goal and framed objectives narrowly around nuclear, naval, and missile capabilities.
It remains unclear whether the 2026 plan was a continuation of concealed 2025-era planning or a distinct decision made later in response to changed battlefield conditions.

theories.steelman: Given that a documented decapitation/regime-change plan was in fact adopted and later executed, the 2025-era public denials of any such intent look, in hindsight, like they understated a real covert objective consistent with the precedent set by the 1953 Ajax coup.

sources.title: Regime change efforts in the 2026 Iran war
How Trump's Iran war objectives have shifted over time
Trump Is Potentially Leading the United States Into an Unnecessary War With Iran
How Trump decided to strike Iran as aides dismissed Netanyahu's regime change plan
Top Trump aides told him PM's prewar regime change forecast was 'farcical' - report

theories.research_note: Unusual case where the 'conspiracy theory' (a hidden broader war plan) was substantiated by subsequent events; scored high accordingly, though the 2025-vs-2026 planning timeline has gaps.

theories.name: Iran War as Petrodollar Defense
theories.summary: Claims that the real driver of US military involvement against Iran was defending the dollar's dominance in global oil trade rather than counter-proliferation, pointing to analyst warnings that the war exposed cracks in the US security guarantee underlying Gulf dollar-oil pricing and reports that Iran collected some wartime tolls in yuan, potentially accelerating a shift toward a 'petroyuan.' Mainstream economic data shows dollar oil pricing remains structurally dominant despite the conflict.

evidence_for.text: Analysts warned the war challenges the US security umbrella for Gulf infrastructure that has underpinned dollar-denominated oil pricing since 1974, risking a shift toward the yuan.
During the conflict Iran was reported to be collecting some tolls or payments in yuan rather than dollars.
Commentary ties the 1974 Saudi petrodollar arrangement directly to US willingness to guarantee Gulf security, framing military intervention as protecting that bargain.

evidence_against.text: IMF analysis found roughly 92% of the mid-2025 decline in dollar reserve share was driven by exchange-rate movements, not active central-bank dumping of dollars.
Saudi Arabia continues to price the overwhelming majority of its oil in dollars, and global oil markets remain structurally dollar-denominated despite the conflict.

sources.title: Dollar dominance is reinforced by the global oil trade, but the Iran war could give rise to the 'petroyuan'
The Iran war puts the petrodollar regime to the test
A Primer on the Petrodollar and the War on Iran
Iran, the $39 trillion national debt and dedollarization

theories.name: Hormuz/Gulf False-Flag Warnings
theories.summary: During the 2026 Iran war, Iranian officials and IRGC representatives repeatedly warned that Israel, possibly with US assistance, would stage attacks on energy infrastructure or shipping in Gulf Cooperation Council states and blame Iran, aiming to drag Gulf states into the war and force Iran to disperse its forces across more fronts. Iran separately denied responsibility for several wartime missile incidents (toward Diego Garcia and Turkey) by calling them Israeli false flags, echoing a similar false-flag dispute over 2019 Gulf tanker attacks.

evidence_for.text: Iranian Foreign Ministry and IRGC officials warned repeatedly that attacks on GCC energy infrastructure could be staged and falsely attributed to Iran to broaden the war.
Iran denied launching missiles at Diego Garcia and at Turkey during the war, describing both incidents as Israeli false flags.
A near-identical false-flag dispute occurred in 2019 when Secretary of State Pompeo accused Iran of tanker attacks that Iran denied, calling them a false flag.

evidence_against.text: Iranian state media has itself been caught making fabricated claims during the war, such as asserting a foreign vessel that ran aground in the Strait was unrelated to Tehran when it was actually tied to Iran, undercutting Iran's credibility as a source on false-flag claims.
No independently verified false-flag attack on Gulf infrastructure has been documented during the 2026 war; the claims remain warnings/accusations rather than confirmed incidents.

sources.title: Shadows of deception: False-flag risks in the Israel–Iran Conflict
2026 Iran war
FACT FOCUS: Iran Claims a Foreign Ship Got Stuck in Strait of Hormuz. but It Is Tied to Tehran

theories.name: Big Oil–OPEC Price-Fixing Collusion
theories.summary: The FTC alleged that former Pioneer Natural Resources CEO Scott Sheffield colluded with OPEC and OPEC+ officials — via hundreds of texts, WhatsApp messages, calls, and in-person meetings — to coordinate reduced US shale output and raise crude and consumer fuel prices, and barred him from an Exxon board seat as a condition of approving Exxon's $64.5 billion Pioneer acquisition, referring the matter to the DOJ for possible criminal antitrust prosecution. Sheffield denies any anticompetitive coordination, calling the characterization of his communications false, and the underlying merger was approved regardless.

evidence_for.text: The FTC's complaint states Sheffield exchanged hundreds of text messages with OPEC representatives and officials discussing crude market dynamics, pricing, and output.
The FTC issued a formal consent order barring Sheffield from Exxon's board or any advisory role to it, specifically citing collusion risk.
The case was referred to the Department of Justice for potential criminal antitrust prosecution.
An economic analysis attributed over 25% of 2021's oil-driven inflation increase to alleged crude price-fixing schemes.

evidence_against.text: Sheffield's lawyers dispute the FTC's characterization, saying he sent only one text to an OPEC official to connect a former OPEC Secretary General with the Texas Railroad Commission for an official government proceeding.
The FTC approved the Exxon-Pioneer merger despite the allegations, and as of mid-2026 no criminal charges had been filed against Sheffield.

sources.title: FTC Order Bans Former Pioneer CEO from Exxon Board Seat in Exxon-Pioneer Deal
FTC accuses ex-Pioneer CEO of colluding with OPEC, blocks him from Exxon board
Is Scott Sheffield a Saudi Collaborator or Political Scapegoat?
Price-fixing case shows need for aggressive Big Oil investigations

theories.research_note: Unusually well-documented for this batch: a formal FTC consent order and complaint exist, though no criminal conviction has resulted as of research date.

theories.name: 'Petrodollar Agreement Expired' Hoax
theories.summary: A viral June 2024 social media claim asserted that a secret 50-year 'petrodollar agreement' obligating Saudi Arabia to sell oil exclusively in US dollars expired on June 9, 2024 and would not be renewed, supposedly heralding an imminent collapse of dollar dominance.
